🩺 Clinical Reference, Interpretations & Examinations

Med Students Assistant
Interpretations & Clinical Examinations
Made Easy

📊 Interpretations Made Easy
🩺 Clinical Examinations Made Easy
🖥
CT Brain Interpretation
Non-contrast & contrast CT — systematic 12-step approach
01
Confirm Patient & Scan Details
Before you look at a single image
▾
Always verify first
Patient ID
Name, DOB, MRN — right patient?
Clinical Indication
Trauma? Stroke? Headache? Seizure? Changes your search pattern.
Date & Time
Compare with priors. Timing matters for infarct/bleed evolution.
Contrast?
NCCT = acute blood/trauma. CE-CT = infection, tumour, abscess.
CT-specific checks
  • 📐Slice thickness: 5mm standard; 1–1.5mm for posterior fossa / skull base
  • 🔧Note window settings — you must adjust them yourself (brain + subdural + bone)
  • ✂️Coverage: vertex to foramen magnum? Posterior fossa fully included?
02
Set Your Windows — All Three, Every Time
The lens through which you see everything
▾
CT Window Settings
🧠 Brain W:70–80 / L:35
Cortex, WM/GM differentiation, edema, parenchymal hemorrhage
🩸 Subdural W:150–200 / L:75
Subtle/isodense SDH, thin extra-axial collections — NEVER skip!
💀 Bone W:2000–4000 / L:700
Fractures, skull lesions, calcification, sinuses
⚠️ Isodense SDH (1–3 weeks old) is invisible on brain window. Only the subdural window reveals it. Look for sulcal effacement and white matter "buckling."
Hounsfield Unit Reference
TissueHU RangeAppearance
Air−1000Black
Fat−100 to −50Dark
CSF / Water0–10Dark grey
White matter20–30Light grey
Grey matter37–45Slightly lighter
Acute blood50–80Bright white
Calcification100–1000Very bright
Bone400–1000Brilliant white
03
Midline Shift & Mass Effect
Do this BEFORE detailed parenchyma review
▾
Measuring Midline Shift
  • 📍Use the septum pellucidum as your midline marker (at the level of foramen of Monro)
  • 📏Measure perpendicular distance from the falx (true midline) to the septum pellucidum
  • 🚨≥5mm = significant — surgical consult needed. Identify direction
  • 🔄Shift TOWARD lesion = atrophy/old injury. Shift AWAY = active mass/edema/hemorrhage
Mass Effect Signs

Sulcal Effacement

Loss of cortical sulci = raised ICP or local edema compressing subarachnoid space

Ventricular Compression

Ipsilateral ventricle compressed/shifted. Check all ventricles — lateral, 3rd, 4th

Basal Cistern Effacement

Loss of suprasellar/perimesencephalic cisterns = critically raised ICP → impending herniation

GW Differentiation Loss

Blurring of grey-white junction = early cytotoxic edema (infarct). Subtle — easy to miss!

🚨 Herniation patterns:
• Subfalcine — cingulate under falx
• Uncal — medial temporal through tentorial notch → CN3 palsy, blown pupil
• Central transtentorial — bilateral downward shift
• Tonsillar — cerebellar tonsils through foramen magnum → Cushing's triad
04
Ventricles & CSF Spaces
Hydrocephalus, IVH, obstruction
▾
Check each ventricle in order
Lateral Ventricles
Frontal, temporal, occipital horns. IVH? Asymmetry? Dilated temporal horns = hydrocephalus
3rd Ventricle
Slit-like normally. Enlarged = downstream block. Check for colloid cyst!
4th Ventricle
Diamond-shaped. Compressed = posterior fossa SOL. Enlarged = outflow obstruction
Aqueduct of Sylvius
Best on sagittal MRI. Stenosis = 3rd+lateral dilated, 4th NORMAL
Hydrocephalus Types
  • 🔒Obstructive: Block at aqueduct/4th V → only upstream ventricles dilate
  • 🌊Communicating: Block at arachnoid granulations → ALL ventricles dilate. Causes: SAH, meningitis, NPH
  • 📉Ex-vacuo: Dilated ventricles + wide sulci = atrophy. NOT true hydrocephalus
  • ⚠️NPH triad: Wet + Wobbly + Wacky + disproportionate ventriculomegaly
💡 Periventricular lucency (hypodensity on CT) = transependymal CSF seepage = ACTIVE hydrocephalus with raised pressure
05
Hemorrhage — Know Every Type Cold
Location, shape, density, and cause
▾
TypeCT AppearanceLocationKey Cause
EDHBiconvex hyperdenseSkull–dura interfaceMMA rupture, temporal # fracture
SDHCrescent hyperdenseDura → arachnoidBridging veins, elderly/trauma
SAHBright sulci/cisternsSubarachnoid spaceAneurysm rupture, AVM, trauma
IPHRound/irregular hyperdenseBrain parenchymaHTN, AVM, amyloid, tumour
IVHHyperdense cast in ventricleVentricular systemExtension of IPH or SAH
⚠️ EDH does NOT cross suture lines (dura adherent). SDH DOES cross sutures but NOT the falx/tentorium. Use this to distinguish them.
🚨 EDH = arterial bleed → expands RAPIDLY. Lucid interval then sudden deterioration. Neurosurgery immediately regardless of current GCS!
06
Brain Parenchyma — Lobe by Lobe
Never skip a region — be systematic
▾
Review order
  • 1️⃣Frontal lobes — ACA territory, white matter changes, subfrontal contusions
  • 2️⃣Parietal lobes — MCA territory, watershed zones
  • 3️⃣Temporal lobes — MCA territory, contusions, uncal herniation
  • 4️⃣Occipital lobes — PCA territory, visual cortex, coup-contrecoup
  • 5️⃣Basal ganglia & thalami — deep perforators, hypertensive IPH, lacunar infarcts
  • 6️⃣Internal capsule / corona radiata — watershed, small vessel disease
  • 7️⃣Cerebellum — PICA/AICA territory, metastases very common here!
  • 8️⃣Brainstem — Basilar perforators, DAI, Duret hemorrhages
Edema patterns

Vasogenic

White matter "fingers." BBB breakdown. Mass lesion / met / abscess

Cytotoxic

Grey + white matter in vascular territory. Cell death. Infarction

Interstitial

Periventricular halo. CSF seeping through ependyma. Hydrocephalus

07
Vascular Signs & Vessels
Don't miss the hyperdense vessel signs
▾
  • 🔴Hyperdense MCA sign: Spontaneous hyperdensity in MCA = acute thrombus → ischemic stroke → urgent CTA + thrombectomy assessment
  • 🔴Dense basilar sign: Hyperdense basilar artery = basilar thrombosis → catastrophic if missed. High mortality
  • ⭕SAH in basal cisterns → aneurysm until proven otherwise → CTA immediately
  • 🌀Vessel wall calcification (atherosclerosis) = common incidental finding
  • 🔵Dense sinus sign: Hyperdense dural sinus = CVST (dural sinus thrombosis) → confirm with CTV/MRV
🚨 Missing the hyperdense MCA sign = missed acute stroke. Deliberately check every MCA when stroke is on the differential — even subtle hyperdensity counts.
08
Posterior Fossa & Brainstem
Most commonly missed — especially on CT
▾
⚠️ Severe beam hardening artifact from petrous bones on CT. Small lesions are often invisible. MRI is always superior for posterior fossa pathology!
What to look for
  • 🔵Cerebellum: PICA infarct = wedge-shaped. Swelling → fatal herniation → urgent posterior fossa decompression
  • 🧠Brainstem: Pontine hemorrhage (HTN) = bilateral "butterfly" hyperdensity. Catastrophic prognosis
  • 📐4th ventricle: Compressed = posterior fossa mass. Expanded + child = medulloblastoma. Adult = ependymoma
  • ⬇️Foramen magnum: Cerebellar tonsils >5mm below FM = Chiari I malformation
  • 🔍CPA angle: Acoustic schwannoma most common — ice cream cone filling internal auditory meatus
09
Skull, Sinuses & Orbits
Always use bone window — don't stop at the brain
▾
Skull (bone window)
  • 💀Fractures: Linear (common), depressed (compound?), basilar — fluid in sphenoid = base of skull fracture
  • 🔍Lytic lesions: Myeloma, metastases, Langerhans cell histiocytosis
  • ⬜Sclerotic lesions: Paget's, fibrous dysplasia, osteoblastic mets
Sinuses & Mastoids
  • 💧Sphenoid sinus fluid in trauma = base of skull fracture until proven otherwise
  • 🦻Mastoid opacification + non-filling sigmoid sinus on CTV = otogenic lateral sinus thrombosis (missed diagnosis!)
  • 👁️Proptosis, orbital wall fracture, retrobulbar hemorrhage — check orbital slices carefully
10
Extra-Axial Spaces & Meninges
The space around the brain matters as much as the brain itself
▾
  • 🔴Epidural space: Biconvex collection = EDH. Does NOT cross suture lines
  • 🟠Subdural space: Crescent collection. DOES cross sutures. Does NOT cross falx. Chronic = isodense/hypodense
  • 🟡Subarachnoid: CSF density normally. Hyperdensity = SAH. FLAIR bright = SAH/meningitis on MRI
  • 🟢Sulcal widening: Diffuse = atrophy. Asymmetric = check for adjacent pathology
11
Normal Variants & Incidentals
Know what's normal so you don't over-report
▾
Benign / Normal Findings
  • ✅Pineal calcification — normal in adults. Worry if >1cm or in child <10yrs
  • ✅Choroid plexus calcification — normal in adults
  • ✅Pacchionian granulations — arachnoid granulations indenting dural sinuses — normal
  • ✅Virchow-Robin spaces — follow CSF density/signal on ALL sequences
  • ✅Cavum septum pellucidum — CSF space between septal leaflets — normal variant
🚨 Never Miss These
  • 🚨Hyperdense MCA or basilar artery sign
  • 🚨Effaced basal cisterns — herniation imminent
  • 🚨Unexpected acute hemorrhage in any location
  • 🚨Uncal herniation signs — effaced ipsilateral suprasellar cistern
  • 🚨Non-accidental injury pattern — SDH of different ages in children
12
Formulate Your Report
Systematic conclusion before you write anything
▾
  • 📝Technique: State modality, slices, contrast or not
  • 📝Findings: Go through each region systematically — don't just describe the obvious finding
  • 📝Impression: Lead with the most critical finding. Provide a differential if uncertain
  • 📞Communicate urgent findings immediately — don't wait for a typed report
  • 🔄Recommend follow-up or further imaging if needed (e.g., MRI for posterior fossa, CTA for vessel query)
Report tip: Always end with "Clinical correlation recommended" only when genuinely uncertain — don't use it as a cop-out for every report!
13
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
CT Brain critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ CT Brain — Emergency Quick Reference

Acute StrokeCT: subtle hypodensity + loss of GWJ + hyperdense MCA sign
SAHHyperdense sulci/basal cisterns → CTA immediately
EDHBiconvex, arterial. Lucid interval. Call neurosurgery NOW
SDHCrescent, venous. Acute=bright, chronic=dark. Check subdural window!
Raised ICPEffaced cisterns + sulcal loss → herniation imminent
CVSTDense sinus on CT. Confirm with CTV. Mimics stroke/ICH
Cerebellar InfarctWedge-shape posterior fossa. Can swell → fatal if missed
Pontine HemorrhageBilateral hyperdensity. Catastrophic prognosis. HTN cause
🔮
MRI Brain Interpretation
Multi-sequence systematic approach — T1, T2, FLAIR, DWI, SWI, T1+Gad
01
Confirm Details & Sequences Acquired
Know what you have before interpreting
▾
Field Strength
1.5T vs 3T. Higher field = better resolution but more artifact
Sequences
Which sequences were acquired? Missing DWI = can't exclude acute stroke
Gadolinium
Enhancement = BBB breakdown. Essential for tumour/infection workup
Planes
Axial (BG, ventricles), Coronal (hippocampus), Sagittal (CC, brainstem)
⚠️ MRI is NOT ideal for acute bone injury or hyperacute blood (<6hrs). CT remains superior for these. Know when to use which.
02
Understand Each Sequence — What It Shows
Click each sequence to review its role
▾
T1
T2
FLAIR
DWI / ADC
GRE / SWI
T1 + Gad
T1 — Anatomy sequence
  • ⚪Fat = bright, CSF = dark, WM slightly brighter than GM
  • 🔍Best for: anatomy, subacute blood (met-Hb bright!), fat-containing lesions, post-contrast enhancement
  • 💡T1 bright causes: fat, subacute hemorrhage, melanin, gadolinium, calcification (sometimes), proteinaceous fluid
  • ⚫T1 dark causes: CSF, water, necrosis, most tumours (at baseline)
T2 — Pathology detector
  • 💧CSF = bright, pathology usually bright (edema, gliosis, tumour, demyelination)
  • 🔍Best for: edema, demyelination, ischemia (subacute), tumours, cysts, syrinx
  • ⚫T2 dark causes: acute blood (deoxyhemoglobin), calcification, melanin, air, dense cellularity (lymphoma, meningioma)
  • ⚠️T2 is sensitive but not specific — nearly every pathology is bright on T2
FLAIR — T2 with CSF suppressed
  • 🌊Like T2 but CSF is nulled (dark) — lesions near CSF become visible
  • 🔍Best for: periventricular MS plaques, cortical lesions, SAH (bright sulci!), encephalitis, hippocampal signal change
  • ⚠️FLAIR bright sulci = SAH, meningitis, leptomeningeal carcinomatosis, or high O₂ (ICU patients)
  • 💡DWI + FLAIR mismatch in stroke: DWI positive + FLAIR negative = infarct <4.5 hours (thrombolysis window!)
DWI / ADC — Diffusion restriction
  • ⚡DWI bright + ADC dark = TRUE restricted diffusion = ACUTE INFARCT (within minutes of onset!)
  • ⚠️T2 shine-through trap: DWI bright + ADC BRIGHT = NOT true restriction — old T2 lesion. ALWAYS check ADC!
  • 🔍DWI restriction also in: abscess (pus restricts strongly), epidermoid cyst, lymphoma, CJD (cortical ribboning), hypercellular tumours
  • 🕐Stroke DWI positivity: within minutes → up to ~7–10 days. After that may normalise (pseudonormalisation)
  • 💊DWI + FLAIR mismatch → infarct within 4.5h window → eligible for thrombolysis (wake-up stroke protocol)
GRE / SWI — Susceptibility / blood products
  • ⚫Sensitive to paramagnetic substances → dark "blooming" artifact (appears larger than true size)
  • 🔍Best for: microbleeds (HTN, CAA, DAI), cavernomas, venous malformations, calcification vs blood
  • 💡SWI is more sensitive than GRE — preferred for microhemorrhage detection
  • 🔄HTN microbleeds: deep, basal ganglia, brainstem. CAA microbleeds: lobar/cortical distribution
  • 🧠DAI (diffuse axonal injury): SWI shows punctate bleeds at grey-white junctions, corpus callosum, brainstem
T1 + Gadolinium — Blood-brain barrier breakdown
  • ✨Enhancement = breakdown of blood-brain barrier OR normally no BBB (pituitary, choroid plexus, dura)
  • 💍Ring enhancement DDx: GBM (irregular thick ring), abscess (smooth thin ring + DWI bright inside), metastasis, tumefactive MS (open ring — incomplete ring), subacute infarct
  • 🌿Leptomeningeal enhancement: sulcal/cortical pattern → bacterial meningitis, TB, carcinomatosis, sarcoid
  • 📏Pachymeningeal (dural) enhancement: intracranial hypotension, SDH, dural mets, rheumatoid pachymeningitis
  • ⚠️Subacute infarct enhances (luxury perfusion, BBB breakdown) — don't mistake for tumour. Check DWI + timeline
03
Midline Shift, Mass Effect & Herniation
Same rules as CT — assess before parenchyma detail
▾
  • 📍Septum pellucidum displacement from midline = midline shift. ≥5mm = significant
  • 💧Sulcal effacement, ventricular compression, basal cistern loss = mass effect / raised ICP
  • 🔴Subfalcine: Cingulate gyrus under falx on coronal T2/FLAIR
  • 🔴Uncal: Medial temporal herniation on coronal T2 → effaced suprasellar cistern + CN3 stretch → ipsilateral blown pupil
  • 🔴Tonsillar: Cerebellar tonsils below foramen magnum on sagittal T1 — >5mm = Chiari I
04
Blood Products — Dating Hemorrhage on MRI
Every stage has a distinct T1/T2 signal pattern
▾
StageTimeframeProductT1T2GRE/SWI
Hyperacute<6 hrsOxyhemoglobinIso/DarkBrightVariable
Acute6h – 3dDeoxyhemoglobinIso/DarkDarkDark bloom
Early subacute3 – 7dIntracellular met-HbBrightDarkDark bloom
Late subacute1 – 4 wkExtracellular met-HbBrightBrightDark bloom
Chronic>4 wkHemosiderinDarkDarkDark bloom
💎 Memory aid: "BIRD" — Bright In subacute (T1). GRE/SWI always blooms dark for any blood product — the most sensitive sequence for microbleeds and old hemorrhage.
05
Ventricles, White Matter & Parenchyma
Systematic lobe-by-lobe review on all sequences
▾
White matter lesions — FLAIR is key
  • 🔵Periventricular + juxtacortical + infratentorial: MS plaques (Dawson's fingers = perpendicular to ventricles on sagittal FLAIR)
  • ⚪Subcortical small vessel disease: Confluent periventricular WM changes, lacunar infarcts in BG/thalamus
  • 🌊CADASIL: Anterior temporal pole + external capsule WM changes + family history
  • 🧠Corpus callosum lesions: MS (Dawson's fingers on sagittal), lymphoma, PRES, trauma (DAI)
Hippocampus (coronal T2 / FLAIR)
  • 📐Mesial temporal sclerosis (MTS): unilateral hippocampal atrophy + T2/FLAIR signal increase + loss of internal architecture
  • 🔥Limbic encephalitis (HSV, anti-NMDA): bilateral medial temporal FLAIR hyperintensity
06
Lesion Characterisation on MRI
A systematic approach to any brain lesion
▾
For every lesion, answer these questions
  • 📍Location: Intra-axial vs extra-axial? Which lobe? Deep vs cortical? Which vascular territory?
  • 🔢Number: Single (primary tumour, abscess, tumefactive MS) vs multiple (mets, MS, septic emboli, cavernomas)
  • 📏Margins: Well-defined (met, abscess) vs infiltrative (HGG, lymphoma, encephalitis)
  • 💧Edema: Extensive vasogenic = met/HGG. Minimal = LGG, cavernoma
  • ✨Enhancement pattern: Solid, ring, nodular, no enhancement?
  • ⚡DWI: Restricts? (Abscess, lymphoma, hypercellular tumour)
  • ⬛SWI: Blooms? (Cavernoma, calcification, hemorrhagic met, AVM)
Ring enhancing lesion DDx

Abscess

Smooth THIN ring. DWI bright inside (pus restricts!). Surrounding edema. Fever + source

GBM / HGG

THICK irregular ring. No DWI restriction inside. Infiltrative margins. Crosses corpus callosum

Metastasis

Well-defined ring. Grey-white junction location. Multiple lesions. Known primary

Tumefactive MS

OPEN ring (incomplete). DWI at active margin. Young patient. Other MS lesions

07
Posterior Fossa, Brainstem & Vessels
MRI is the gold standard here — use it
▾
  • 🌊Flow void: Normal arteries = dark on T1/T2. Loss of flow void = thrombus or slow flow (CVST, AVM nidus)
  • ⚡MRA (TOF): Circle of Willis, aneurysm, stenosis, occlusion. No contrast needed
  • 🔵MRV: Dural venous sinuses. Filling defect = CVST. Superior sagittal sinus most commonly affected
  • 🧠Brainstem: Small lacunar infarcts, central pontine myelinolysis (CPM — DWI + T2 bright trident sign), brainstem glioma in children
  • ⬇️Sagittal T1: Corpus callosum integrity, pituitary, aqueduct, cerebellar tonsil position (Chiari)
💎 CVST tip: DWI may show restricted diffusion in the sinus (thrombus) OR in adjacent parenchyma (venous infarct). Venous infarcts don't respect arterial territories!
08
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
MRI Brain critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ MRI Brain — Emergency Quick Reference

Acute StrokeDWI bright + ADC dark within minutes. DWI+FLAIR mismatch = <4.5h window
SAHFLAIR bright sulci. CT may be negative after 24h. MRI FLAIR more sensitive late
AbscessDWI bright INSIDE ring = pus. Thin smooth ring enhancement
EncephalitisFLAIR bright medial temporal (HSV). Check DWI. Urgent LP
CVSTLoss of flow void in sinus. Filling defect on MRV. Venous infarct ≠ arterial territory
Tumefactive MSOpen ring enhancement. Young patient. DWI at margins
GBMThick irregular ring. Crosses CC. No DWI inside. Infiltrative
MicrobleedsSWI/GRE blooming. HTN=deep. CAA=lobar. DAI=GWJ/CC
❤️
ECG Interpretation
Fully systematic 10-step approach — rate, rhythm, axis, intervals, morphology, diagnosis
01
Confirm Details & Calibration
Before reading a single wave
▾
Patient & Date
Name, DOB, date of recording. Compare with previous ECGs always.
Standard Speed
25 mm/sec (standard). Each small box = 0.04s. Each large box = 0.2s.
Calibration
Standard = 10mm/mV. Check calibration marker at start of trace.
Lead Quality
Any leads off? Artifact? Tremor baseline? Can't interpret poor quality leads.
Standard ECG layout — 12 leads
I
Lateral
II
Inferior
III
Inferior
aVR
Cavity
aVL
Lateral
aVF
Inferior
V1
Septal
V2
Septal
V3
Anterior
V4
Anterior
V5
Lateral
V6
Lateral
🔑 Territory groupings to memorise: Inferior = II, III, aVF (RCA). Lateral = I, aVL, V5, V6 (LCx). Anterior = V1–V4 (LAD). Septal = V1, V2.
02
Heart Rate
Multiple methods — know them all
▾
Rate classification
<60
Bradycardia
60–100
Normal
>100
Tachycardia
Methods to calculate rate
  • 1️⃣300 ÷ large boxes between R waves — quick and classic. Memorise: 300, 150, 100, 75, 60, 50 for 1–6 large boxes
  • 2️⃣1500 ÷ small boxes between R waves — more precise at slower rates
  • 3️⃣Count complexes in a 6-second strip × 10 — best for irregular rhythms (AF)
  • 4️⃣Machine-calculated rate is a starting point only — always confirm yourself
⚠️ In atrial fibrillation, the "rate" is a range — report as e.g. "ventricular rate 70–110 bpm." Never use the 300÷ method for irregular rhythms.
03
Rhythm Analysis
Regular or irregular? P before every QRS?
▾
Step-by-step rhythm analysis
  • 1️⃣Is it regular or irregular? Use calipers/pencil to mark R-R intervals across a long strip
  • 2️⃣Is there a P wave before every QRS? Normal sinus: P before every QRS, same morphology
  • 3️⃣Is every P followed by a QRS? If not → heart block
  • 4️⃣P wave morphology: Upright in I, II, aVF, inverted in aVR = sinus origin. Absent = AF. Sawtooth = flutter. Multiple morphologies = WAP/MAT
  • 5️⃣QRS width: Narrow <0.12s (supraventricular). Wide ≥0.12s (ventricular or aberrant conduction)
Common rhythms — quick ID
RhythmRateP wavesQRSKey feature
Normal sinus60–100Before each QRSNarrowRegular, upright P in II
Sinus bradycardia<60NormalNarrowSlow but organised
Sinus tachycardia>100NormalNarrowGradual onset, cause-driven
Atrial fibrillationVariableAbsent (fibrillatory)Narrow (usu)Irregularly irregular!
Atrial flutter150 (2:1)Sawtooth 300/minNarrowConstant 2:1 most common
SVT (AVNRT)150–250Hidden in QRSNarrowSudden onset/offset
VT>100DissociatedWide ≥0.12sAV dissociation, fusion beats
VFChaoticNoneChaoticNo pulse, defibrillate NOW
Junctional40–60Inverted / absentNarrowRetrograde P or buried
🚨 Wide complex tachycardia = VT until proven otherwise. Treat as VT if in doubt. Giving verapamil to VT can be fatal. Never assume SVT with aberrancy first.
04
Cardiac Axis
The direction of the main electrical vector
▾
Normal axis = −30° to +90°
Normal Axis
−30° to +90°
I: positive, aVF: positive
Left Axis Deviation
−30° to −90°
I: positive, aVF: negative
Right Axis Deviation
>+90°
I: negative, aVF: positive
Extreme Axis
−90° to ±180°
I: negative, aVF: negative
Quick axis check — just use leads I and aVF
  • ✅Both positive = normal axis (most common). No further calculation needed
  • ⬅️Lead I positive + aVF negative = LEFT axis. Causes: LBBB, LAFB, inferior MI, LVH, pre-excitation, pacemaker
  • ➡️Lead I negative + aVF positive = RIGHT axis. Causes: RBBB, LPFB, RVH, PE (acute), Dextrocardia, lateral MI, normal in children
  • ↕️Both negative = extreme/northwest axis. Causes: VT, pacemaker, severe cardiomyopathy, hyperkalemia
Memory trick: Think of a thumbs up/down in each lead — up = positive QRS, down = negative. If both thumbs up = normal. I up + aVF down = left. I down + aVF up = right.
05
P Wave Analysis
Atrial depolarisation — small but critical
▾
Normal P wave
  • 📏Duration <0.12s (3 small boxes), amplitude <2.5mm
  • ⬆️Upright in I, II, aVF, V4–V6. Inverted in aVR. Variable in III, aVL, V1
  • 🔄One P per QRS in normal sinus rhythm
P wave abnormalities
FindingDescriptionCause
P mitraleBroad, notched P in II (M-shaped), biphasic in V1Left atrial enlargement (mitral stenosis, LVH)
P pulmonaleTall, peaked P in II (>2.5mm)Right atrial enlargement (pulmonary HTN, COPD, TS)
Absent P wavesFibrillatory baselineAtrial fibrillation
Sawtooth PRegular 300/min flutter wavesAtrial flutter
Inverted P in IINegative P in inferior leadsJunctional rhythm, retrograde conduction
Multiple morphologies≥3 different P shapesMAT (multifocal atrial tachycardia) — COPD
06
PR Interval & AV Conduction
The AV node is a traffic light — check it every time
▾
PR interval normal = 0.12–0.20s (3–5 small boxes)
FindingPR IntervalRhythmDiagnosis
Normal0.12–0.20sRegularNormal sinus rhythm
Short PR<0.12sRegularWPW (delta wave!), LGL, junctional, AV nodal bypass
Long PR (fixed)>0.20sRegular, 1:11st degree AV block
Progressively long → dropped QRSLengtheningGrouped beating2nd degree Mobitz I (Wenckebach)
Fixed long → sudden dropped QRSConstantGrouped beating2nd degree Mobitz II — HIGH RISK
No relationship P to QRSVariableAV dissociation3rd degree (complete) AV block
🚨 Mobitz II and 3rd degree AV block can progress to asystole. Both require urgent pacing assessment regardless of haemodynamic stability. Never dismiss these as "just a heart block."
WPW — Short PR + Delta Wave
  • ⚡Short PR + slurred QRS upstroke (delta wave) + wide QRS = Wolff-Parkinson-White syndrome
  • 🚨Risk of AF conducting rapidly via accessory pathway → VF. AVOID AV nodal blocking agents (verapamil, adenosine, digoxin)!
07
QRS Complex Analysis
Ventricular depolarisation — bundle branches, hypertrophy, MI
▾
Normal QRS = narrow <0.12s, normal progression V1→V6
Width / BBB
Hypertrophy
R Progression
Pathological Q
Bundle Branch Blocks

RBBB — "Rabbit ears" in V1

  • • QRS ≥0.12s
  • • rSR' in V1 (M-shaped / "rabbit ears")
  • • Wide, slurred S in I, V5, V6
  • • New RBBB + anterior ST elevation = Brugada!

LBBB — "WiLLiaM" pattern

  • • QRS ≥0.12s
  • • Broad notched R in I, aVL, V5, V6 (W in V1, M in V6)
  • • Discordant ST changes — expected, not ischaemia
  • • New LBBB + chest pain = treat as STEMI equivalent (Sgarbossa criteria)
🚨 New LBBB + acute chest pain = STEMI equivalent. Activate cath lab. Apply Sgarbossa criteria if needed: concordant ST elevation ≥1mm in any lead OR ST depression ≥1mm in V1–V3 = very specific for MI.
Ventricular Hypertrophy
FeatureLVHRVH
AxisNormal or LADRAD (>+90°)
QRS morphologyTall R in V5/V6, deep S in V1/V2Tall R in V1, deep S in V5/V6
Sokolow-Lyon (LVH)SV1 + RV5/V6 >35mm—
ST-T changesST depression + T inversion in lateral leads (strain)ST depression + T inversion in V1–V3
CausesHTN, AS, HCM, ARPulm HTN, COPD, PE (acute)
R Wave Progression V1 → V6
  • 📈Normal: R wave grows from V1 to V6. Transition (R=S) normally at V3 or V4
  • ⬅️Poor R wave progression (PRWP): Transition delayed (V4+). Causes: anterior MI, LBBB, LVH, COPD, cardiomyopathy
  • ↩️Reverse R progression: R decreases from V1 onwards. Strongly suggests anterior MI
  • ⚡Tall R in V1 alone: RBBB, WPW type A, posterior MI, RVH, HCM, Duchenne MD
Pathological Q Waves = Myocardial Infarction (Old)
  • 📏Pathological Q wave criteria: Duration ≥0.04s (1 small box) AND depth ≥25% of following R wave height
  • 🔍Must be present in ≥2 contiguous leads in the same territory
  • 📍Inferior: Q waves in II, III, aVF = inferior MI (RCA)
  • 📍Anterior: Loss of R wave / Q in V1–V4 = anterior MI (LAD)
  • 📍Lateral: Q in I, aVL = lateral MI (LCx)
  • ⚠️Q in III and aVR alone = normal variant. Needs to be in ≥2 contiguous leads
08
ST Segment Analysis — STEMI vs NSTEMI
The most critical step for acute management
▾
Normal ST segment = isoelectric (flat at baseline)
ST Elevation
ST Depression
STEMI Mimics
STEMI criteria
  • 📏≥2mm elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads in V2–V3 (≥2.5mm in men <40yrs, ≥1.5mm in women)
  • 📏≥1mm elevation in ≥2 contiguous leads in all other leads
TerritoryLeads with STEArteryReciprocal Changes
Inferior STEMIII, III, aVFRCA (80%) / LCx (20%)ST depression in I, aVL
Anterior STEMIV1–V4LAD (proximal)ST depression in II, III, aVF
Lateral STEMII, aVL, V5, V6LCx / diagonalST depression in II, III, aVF
Posterior STEMIST DEPRESSION V1–V3!RCA / LCxTall R in V1, upright T in V1–V3
Right ventricularV1, V4R elevationRCA (proximal)Always check with inferior STEMI
🚨 Posterior MI is the most missed STEMI! Look for ST DEPRESSION in V1–V3 + tall R in V1 + upright T in V1. Do posterior leads (V7–V9) to confirm: STE ≥0.5mm in V7–V9 = posterior STEMI.
🚨 RV infarction with inferior STEMI: Always do right-sided leads (V4R). Avoid GTN/nitrates and diuretics — the RV is preload-dependent. Give IV fluids instead.
ST Depression — causes and patterns
PatternShapeCause
Horizontal / downslopingFlat or droops downIschaemia / NSTEMI
UpslopingSlopes upwardLess specific — tachycardia, physiological
J-point depression onlyRapid return to baselineNormal variant, especially with tachycardia
Lateral ST depression + T inversionLateral leadsLVH strain pattern
Reciprocal changesMirror of STE territorySTEMI in opposite territory
Digoxin effect"Reverse tick" / scoopedDigoxin therapy (not toxicity per se)
STEMI mimics — know these cold!
MimicECG FeaturesHow to differentiate
Early repolarisationSTE in V2–V5 with J-point notchingConcave STE, fishook J-point. Young, athletic. No reciprocal changes
PericarditisDiffuse saddle-shaped STE, PR depressionInvolves ALL leads. No reciprocal changes. PR depression (pathognomonic)
LBBB (new)Wide QRS + concordant STEApply Sgarbossa criteria. Treat as STEMI if concordant changes
LVHSTE in V1–V3Discordant to QRS direction. Tall QRS voltages present
BrugadaRBBB + coved STE in V1–V2Type 1 Brugada pattern. Risk of VF. Genetic. No pain
aVR STESTE in aVR + diffuse STDLeft main / proximal LAD occlusion or severe 3-vessel disease
HyperkalaemiaPeaked T, wide QRS, sine waveNo chest pain pattern. Peaked T earliest sign. Check K+
STEMI-equivalent: De WinterUpsloping STD in V1–V6 + tall T wavesProximal LAD occlusion! No STE. Activate cath lab
09
T Wave & QT Interval
Repolarisation abnormalities and dangerous QT prolongation
▾
Normal T waves
  • ⬆️Upright in I, II, V3–V6. Inverted in aVR. Variable in III, aVL, V1
  • 📏Amplitude <5mm in limb leads, <10mm in precordial leads
  • 🔄Same direction as QRS (concordant)
T wave abnormalities
FindingLeadsCause
Peaked / tall T wavesAnyHyperkalemia (earliest sign), hyperacute STEMI, early repolarisation
Deep symmetrical T inversionV1–V4Anterior ischaemia, NSTEMI, Wellens syndrome (proximal LAD stenosis!)
Deep symmetrical T inversionV1–V6Apical HCM (Yamaguchi pattern), large PE, NSTEMI
T inversion II, III, aVFInferiorInferior ischaemia, RV strain (PE, COPD)
Biphasic T waves in V2–V3V2–V3Wellens type A — reperfusing proximal LAD stenosis. High risk of anterior STEMI!
Flattened T wavesAnyNon-specific — hypokalemia, LVH, digitalis
QT Interval — corrected QTc
  • 📏Measure from start of QRS to end of T wave in V5 or II (clearest)
  • 🧮Bazett formula: QTc = QT ÷ √RR interval (RR in seconds)
  • ✅Normal QTc: <440ms (men), <460ms (women)
  • 🚨QTc >500ms = high risk of Torsades de Pointes (polymorphic VT). Stop offending drugs!
Causes of prolonged QT

Drugs (most common)

Amiodarone, sotalol, quinidine, haloperidol, methadone, macrolide antibiotics, fluconazole, chloroquine

Electrolytes

Hypokalaemia, hypomagnesaemia, hypocalcaemia — all prolong QT. Check and correct urgently.

Congenital LQTS

Romano-Ward (AD), Jervell-Lange-Nielsen (AR + deafness). Young patient, syncope, family history of sudden death

Acquired

Myocarditis, hypothyroidism, hypothermia, head injury (raised ICP), subarachnoid hemorrhage

🚨 Wellens syndrome = pre-infarction pattern. Biphasic or deep inverted T in V2–V3, often with minimal pain, may be pain-free at time of ECG. This patient has critical proximal LAD stenosis. Do NOT do stress test — urgent cath!
10
Formulate Your ECG Report
Put it all together — systematic conclusion every time
▾
Structured report format
  • 1️⃣Rate: "Heart rate approximately ___ bpm"
  • 2️⃣Rhythm: "Normal sinus rhythm / AF / VT etc."
  • 3️⃣Axis: "Normal axis / LAD / RAD"
  • 4️⃣Intervals: "PR ___ ms, QRS ___ ms, QTc ___ ms. [Abnormal if prolonged/short]"
  • 5️⃣P waves: "Normal / P mitrale / P pulmonale / absent"
  • 6️⃣QRS: "Narrow / wide. RBBB/LBBB. Pathological Q waves in [territory]. Poor R wave progression."
  • 7️⃣ST segment: "STE in [leads] consistent with [territory] STEMI / STD in [leads] suggesting ischaemia"
  • 8️⃣T waves: "T inversion in [leads]. Peaked T. Biphasic T in V2–V3 (Wellens)."
  • 9️⃣Overall impression: "This ECG is consistent with [diagnosis]. Clinical correlation required."
  • 📞Urgent action if needed: Call immediately for STEMI, VT, complete heart block, Torsades
Always compare with the old ECG! Dynamic change (new LBBB, new STE, new deep T inversion) matters far more than an isolated finding in isolation. An ECG that "looks normal" may be massively abnormal for that patient.
11
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
ECG critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ ECG — Emergency Quick Reference

STEMISTE ≥1mm in ≥2 contiguous leads. Activate cath lab NOW
Posterior MISTD in V1–V3 + tall R in V1. Do posterior leads. Easily missed!
RV InfarctInferior STEMI + STE in V4R. No nitrates! Give fluids
VTWide complex tachy = VT until proven. Never give verapamil!
VFChaotic trace. No pulse. Defibrillate immediately. No other option
Complete Heart BlockP waves dissociated from QRS. Urgent pacing assessment
TorsadesQTc >500ms + polymorphic VT. Stop QT-prolonging drugs. Mg2+ IV
WPW + AFIrregularly irregular + wide QRS + delta waves. Avoid AV blockers! Procainamide
Wellens SyndromeBiphasic/deep T inv in V2–V3. Critical LAD stenosis. No stress test!
De Winter T wavesUpsloping STD + tall T in V1–V6. Proximal LAD occlusion. Activate cath!
HyperkalaemiaPeaked T → wide QRS → sine wave → VF. Calcium gluconate first!
aVR STESTE in aVR + diffuse STD = left main / proximal LAD. Highest mortality
🫁
ABG Interpretation
Arterial Blood Gas — systematic 6-step approach: pH → ventilation → oxygenation → compensation → anion gap → clinical context
pH
7.35 – 7.45
PaCO₂
35 – 45 mmHg
HCO₃⁻
22 – 26 mEq/L
PaO₂
80 – 100 mmHg
SpO₂
>94%
Base Excess
−2 to +2
01
Step 1 — Assess the pH
Is it acidaemia, alkalaemia, or normal?
▾
pH classification
Acidaemia
pH < 7.35
Primary process is an acidosis
Normal
pH 7.35 – 7.45
Can still have mixed disorders!
Alkalaemia
pH > 7.45
Primary process is an alkalosis
⚠️ A normal pH does NOT mean no disorder — compensated or mixed disorders can have a normal pH. Always analyse all components regardless of pH.
  • 🔑pH <7.2 = severe acidaemia → life-threatening. Urgent management
  • 🔑pH >7.6 = severe alkalaemia → risk of arrhythmias, tetany, seizures
  • 💡The pH tells you the dominant disorder when two processes coexist
02
Step 2 — Identify the Primary Disorder
Is it respiratory or metabolic? Check PaCO₂ and HCO₃⁻
▾
The 4 primary disorders
DisorderpHPaCO₂HCO₃⁻Primary driver
Respiratory Acidosis↓↑ >45Normal/↑Hypoventilation → CO₂ retention
Respiratory Alkalosis↑↓ <35Normal/↓Hyperventilation → CO₂ washout
Metabolic Acidosis↓Normal/↓↓ <22HCO₃⁻ loss or acid gain
Metabolic Alkalosis↑Normal/↑↑ >26HCO₃⁻ gain or acid loss
Key rule: The parameter that moves in the SAME direction as pH = the compensatory change. The one that moves in the OPPOSITE direction = the primary driver.
03
Step 3 — Assess Compensation
Is the compensation appropriate? Is there a second disorder hiding?
▾
Expected compensation formulas
Primary DisorderExpected CompensationIf Actual ≠ Expected
Metabolic AcidosisPaCO₂ = (1.5 × HCO₃⁻) + 8 ± 2
(Winter's formula)		Higher PaCO₂ = concurrent resp acidosis
Lower PaCO₂ = concurrent resp alkalosis
Metabolic AlkalosisPaCO₂ increases 0.7 mmHg per 1 mEq/L ↑ HCO₃⁻Lower = concurrent resp alkalosis
Higher = concurrent resp acidosis
Respiratory Acidosis (acute)HCO₃⁻ rises 1 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ CO₂More HCO₃⁻ rise = concurrent met alkalosis
Respiratory Acidosis (chronic)HCO₃⁻ rises 3.5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↑ CO₂Chronic COPD — higher HCO₃⁻ expected
Respiratory Alkalosis (acute)HCO₃⁻ falls 2 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↓ CO₂More fall = concurrent met acidosis
Respiratory Alkalosis (chronic)HCO₃⁻ falls 5 mEq/L per 10 mmHg ↓ CO₂Pregnancy, altitude acclimatisation
💡 Compensation is never complete (never brings pH to exactly normal). If pH normalises fully → suspect a mixed disorder, not pure compensation.
04
Step 4 — Anion Gap (if Metabolic Acidosis)
MANDATORY in every metabolic acidosis — narrows the differential dramatically
▾
Anion Gap formula
AG = Na⁺ − (Cl⁻ + HCO₃⁻)
Normal: 8–12 mEq/L (without albumin correction). Corrected AG = AG + 2.5 × (4 − albumin g/dL)

High AG Metabolic Acidosis (>12)

MUDPILES / GOLDMARK:

  • Methanol / Metformin
  • Uraemia (renal failure)
  • DKA / AKA
  • Propylene glycol / Paracetamol
  • Iron / Isoniazid / Inborn errors
  • Lactic acidosis (sepsis, ischaemia, liver failure)
  • Ethylene glycol / Ethanol
  • Salicylates

Normal AG Metabolic Acidosis (8–12)

HARDASS / USED CARP:

  • Hyperalimentation / Hyperchloraemia (saline excess)
  • Addison's disease
  • RTA (Type 1, 2, 4)
  • Diarrhoea (GI HCO₃⁻ loss)
  • Acetazolamide
  • Spironolactone
  • Saline infusion (dilutional)
Delta-delta ratio — unmasking hidden disorders
Δ/Δ = (AG − 12) ÷ (24 − HCO₃⁻)
<1 = mixed high AG + normal AG acidosis  |  1–2 = pure high AG acidosis  |  >2 = high AG acidosis + concurrent metabolic alkalosis
05
Step 5 — Oxygenation Assessment
PaO₂, A-a gradient, and type of respiratory failure
▾
Oxygenation parameters
PaO₂ Normal
80–100 mmHg on room air. Decreases with age: ~100 − (age/4)
PaO₂/FiO₂ ratio
>400 normal. 200–300 = mild ARDS. 100–200 = moderate. <100 = severe ARDS
A-a Gradient
PAO₂ − PaO₂. Normal = Age/4 + 4 mmHg. Elevated = parenchymal/vascular cause
A-a gradient formula: PAO₂ = (FiO₂ × 713) − (PaCO₂ ÷ 0.8)
TypePaO₂PaCO₂A-a gradientCauses
Type 1 Failure↓Normal or ↓ElevatedPE, pneumonia, pulm oedema, ARDS, fibrosis — V/Q mismatch or shunt
Type 2 Failure↓↑ >45NormalCOPD, resp muscle weakness, OHS, opiate excess, CNS depression — pump failure
High altitude / hypoventilation↓↑NormalLow FiO₂ or reduced drive — no parenchymal disease
🚨 PaO₂ <60 mmHg = respiratory failure. PaO₂ <55 mmHg = threshold for long-term oxygen therapy in COPD. PaO₂ <40 mmHg = life-threatening.
06
Step 6 — Common Clinical Scenarios
Putting it all together at the bedside
▾
Classic ABG patterns to recognise instantly
pHPaCO₂HCO₃⁻PatternThink of
7.255523Resp acidosis, acuteAcute severe asthma, opiate overdose, resp arrest
7.325830Resp acidosis, chronicCOPD exacerbation, chronic hypoventilation
7.552824Resp alkalosisAnxiety, PE, early sepsis, pregnancy, altitude
7.20228Met acidosis high AGDKA, lactic acidosis, uraemia, toxic ingestion
7.303014Met acidosis + resp compDiarrhoea, RTA, saline excess (normal AG)
7.504836Met alkalosis + resp compVomiting, NG suctioning, diuretics, Conn's syndrome
7.402012Mixed met acidosis + resp alkSalicylate poisoning (classic!), septic shock
7.385029Compensated resp acidosisStable COPD with chronic CO₂ retention
🔑 Salicylate poisoning classic pattern: Mixed respiratory alkalosis (direct stimulation of respiratory centre) + high AG metabolic acidosis. If both are present = salicylate poisoning until proven otherwise.
07
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
ABG critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ ABG — Quick Reference

pH <7.2Severe acidaemia. Urgent. Risk of haemodynamic collapse and arrhythmia
Type 2 Resp FailurePaO₂↓ + PaCO₂↑. Pump failure. NIV/intubation decision
DKAHigh AG met acidosis + ketones + glucose. AG often >20
Lactic AcidosisHigh AG + lactate >2. Sepsis, ischaemia, metformin, liver failure
COPD ExacerbationResp acidosis. Compare with baseline. Chronic compensation HCO₃⁻ raised
Salicylate PoisoningMixed resp alk + met acidosis. High AG. Classic dual pattern
Vomiting / NG suctionMet alkalosis + hypochloraemia + hypokalaemia
ARDSPaO₂/FiO₂ <300. Bilateral infiltrates. Type 1 failure. Elevated A-a gradient
🫀
Liver Function Tests (LFTs)
Systematic interpretation: hepatocellular vs cholestatic vs mixed — pattern recognition + differential
ALT
7–56 U/L
AST
10–40 U/L
ALP
44–147 U/L
GGT
8–61 U/L
Bilirubin (total)
<21 µmol/L
Albumin
35–50 g/L
Total Protein
60–80 g/L
PT / INR
INR <1.2
01
Identify the Pattern — Hepatocellular vs Cholestatic
This single step directs your entire differential
▾

Hepatocellular Pattern

Predominantly ALT + AST elevated
ALP/GGT mildly raised or normal

R ratio = ALT/ALP (both as ULN multiples)
R > 5 = hepatocellular

Cholestatic Pattern

Predominantly ALP + GGT elevated
ALT/AST mildly raised or normal

R < 2 = cholestatic
R 2–5 = mixed pattern

Degree of ALT/AST elevation
ElevationMagnitudeThink of
Mild<3× ULNNAFLD, medications, alcohol, chronic hepatitis, thyroid disease, coeliac
Moderate3–10× ULNViral hepatitis, autoimmune hepatitis, alcohol, medications, NASH
Severe>10× ULNAcute viral hepatitis, ischaemic hepatitis, drug-induced (paracetamol), autoimmune, acute Budd-Chiari
Massive (>100×)>1000 U/LParacetamol hepatotoxicity, ischaemic hepatitis ("shock liver"), acute viral hepatitis A/B
02
AST : ALT Ratio — The DeRitis Ratio
The ratio often tells you more than the absolute values
▾
AST:ALT RatioPatternDiagnosis to consider
<1 (ALT dominant)ALT > ASTViral hepatitis (A, B, C), NAFLD/NASH, drug-induced hepatitis
≈1EqualNon-specific. Most chronic liver diseases
>2 (AST dominant)AST >> ALTAlcoholic hepatitis (ratio often 2:1 to 3:1). Also: ischaemic hepatitis, Wilson's, cirrhosis
>3AST dominantStrongly suggests alcoholic hepatitis if AST 100–300. GGT also very elevated
Alcoholic hepatitis rule: AST rarely exceeds 300–400 U/L in alcohol even if severe. AST >1000 = another cause (not just alcohol).
💡 ALT is more liver-specific than AST. AST also rises in muscle disease (myocarditis, rhabdomyolysis, MI) — always check CK if AST elevated with normal ALT.
03
ALP & GGT — Cholestatic Markers
Distinguish intra vs extrahepatic cholestasis
▾
ALP — Alkaline Phosphatase
  • 🦴Non-hepatic ALP sources: Bone (Paget's, fractures, metastases, adolescent growth), intestine, kidney, placenta — always check GGT!
  • 🟢GGT elevated with ALP = hepatic/biliary origin confirmed
  • 🟡GGT normal with ALP elevated = bone source. Check ALP isoforms or bone markers
  • 📏ALP >3× ULN with normal ALT/AST = primary biliary cholangitis (PBC), PSC, drug-induced cholestasis, biliary obstruction, bone disease
GGT — Gamma GT
  • 🍺GGT is highly sensitive for alcohol use — rises before other LFTs with regular alcohol intake. Not specific.
  • 💊Also raised by: enzyme-inducing drugs (phenytoin, rifampicin, carbamazepine), fatty liver, any liver disease
  • 🔍GGT isolated elevation: alcohol, drugs, NAFLD — rarely serious in isolation
Cholestasis differential
Intrahepatic CholestasisExtrahepatic Cholestasis
PBC, PSC, viral hepatitis, drugs (co-amoxiclav, flucloxacillin, steroids), alcohol, pregnancy (ICP), sepsis, TPNCholedocholithiasis, cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic cancer head, pancreatitis, Mirizzi syndrome, biliary stricture
04
Bilirubin — Jaundice Classification
Pre-hepatic vs hepatic vs post-hepatic
▾

Pre-hepatic (unconjugated)

Bilirubin↑, conjugated normal
Urine: no bilirubin (not water-soluble)
Urobilinogen↑

Causes: Haemolysis (sickle cell, G6PD, autoimmune), Gilbert's syndrome, ineffective erythropoiesis

Hepatic (mixed)

Both conjugated + unconjugated↑
Urine: bilirubin present
Dark urine + pale stools (if cholestatic component)

Causes: Hepatitis (viral, autoimmune, alcohol), cirrhosis, drugs, sepsis

Post-hepatic (conjugated)

Conjugated bilirubin↑↑
Dark urine + pale stools + pruritus
ALP markedly elevated

Causes: Bile duct stones, pancreatic cancer, cholangiocarcinoma, PSC, Mirizzi

⚠️ Gilbert's syndrome: Isolated unconjugated bilirubin elevation, normal LFTs, triggered by fasting/illness/exercise. Benign. No treatment. Confirm by 5-day fasting test or UGT1A1 mutation.
05
Albumin, PT/INR — Synthetic Function
The true measure of how well the liver is actually working
▾
Markers of synthetic function
  • 🔬Albumin: Made only by the liver. Half-life 20 days. Low albumin = chronic liver disease or malnutrition. Acute liver failure may have normal albumin initially!
  • ⏱️PT/INR: Reflects clotting factor synthesis (factors I, II, V, VII, X — all liver-made). Half-life hours to days. Best acute marker of liver synthetic function
  • 📉Albumin <30 g/L + elevated INR + jaundice = severe liver impairment or acute liver failure
  • ⚠️Low albumin also from: nephrotic syndrome (urinary loss), malnutrition, sepsis (dilution + catabolism), protein-losing enteropathy
Acute liver failure criteria (Kings College Hospital)
Paracetamol ALD
pH <7.3 OR (PT >100s + Cr >300 + grade 3–4 encephalopathy) → transplant listing
Non-paracetamol ALD
PT >100s OR any 3 of: age <10 or >40, non-A non-B hepatitis / drug, jaundice >7d before encephalopathy, PT >50s, bilirubin >300
06
Child-Pugh & MELD Score
Scoring severity of chronic liver disease
▾
Child-Pugh Score
Parameter1 point2 points3 points
Bilirubin<34 µmol/L34–51>51
Albumin>35 g/L28–35<28
PT prolongation<4 sec4–6 sec>6 sec
AscitesNoneMildModerate–severe
EncephalopathyNoneGrade 1–2Grade 3–4
Child A (5–6)
Compensated. 1-yr survival 100%. No major contraindication to surgery
Child B (7–9)
Significant dysfunction. 1-yr survival ~80%. Surgery high risk
Child C (10–15)
Decompensated. 1-yr survival ~45%. Transplant assessment
MELD Score (Model for End-Stage Liver Disease)
MELD = 3.78×ln(bilirubin) + 11.2×ln(INR) + 9.57×ln(Cr) + 6.43
Score >15 = transplant benefit. >25 = high 3-month mortality. >40 = very high mortality. Used for organ allocation.
07
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
LFT critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ LFTs — Quick Reference

Acute Liver FailureEncephalopathy + coagulopathy + jaundice. KCH criteria for transplant
Paracetamol ODALT/AST may reach >10,000. N-acetylcysteine urgently. pH <7.3 = transplant
Ischaemic HepatitisALT/AST >1000 rapidly. History of hypotension/cardiac event. Shock liver
Obstructive JaundiceALP↑↑, conjugated bili↑↑, pale stools, dark urine. Ultrasound first
Alcoholic HepatitisAST:ALT >2, ratio rarely >300, GGT↑↑, raised MCV. Maddrey score
PBCMiddle-aged woman. ALP↑↑, GGT↑, AMA positive. Cholestatic pattern
Gilbert'sIsolated unconjugated bili↑. All other LFTs normal. Benign. No Rx needed
HaemolysisUnconjugated bili↑, LDH↑, haptoglobin↓, DAT positive. Check blood film
🫘
Renal Function Tests (RFTs)
Systematic interpretation: creatinine, urea, GFR, electrolytes, AKI vs CKD staging
Creatinine
60–110 µmol/L (M)
45–90 µmol/L (F)
Urea
2.5–7.5 mmol/L
eGFR
>90 mL/min/1.73m²
Na⁺
135–145 mmol/L
K⁺
3.5–5.0 mmol/L
Bicarbonate
22–29 mmol/L
Urine PCR
<15 mg/mmol
Urine ACR
<3 mg/mmol
01
Creatinine & eGFR — Understand the Numbers
Creatinine is a late marker — GFR can halve before creatinine doubles
▾
eGFR — CKD staging (KDIGO)
StageeGFR (mL/min/1.73m²)DescriptionAction
G1≥90Normal or highTreat if markers of kidney damage present (proteinuria/haematuria)
G260–89Mildly decreasedMonitor annually. Optimize BP/glucose
G3a45–59Mildly-moderately decreasedNephrology referral if declining. Avoid nephrotoxins
G3b30–44Moderately-severely decreasedNephrology referral. Manage complications (anaemia, bone disease)
G415–29Severely decreasedPrepare for RRT. AV fistula creation. Transplant workup
G5<15Kidney failureDialysis/transplant. Uraemic symptoms
💡 Creatinine is heavily influenced by muscle mass. A bodybuilder with Cr 130 µmol/L may have normal GFR. An elderly woman with Cr 90 µmol/L may have Stage 3 CKD. ALWAYS use eGFR, not just creatinine alone.
02
AKI vs CKD — Distinguish Them
Management is completely different — don't confuse them
▾

AKI — Acute Kidney Injury

Rise in Cr ≥26 µmol/L in 48h OR ≥1.5× baseline in 7d OR urine output <0.5 mL/kg/hr for >6h

Kidneys are normal size on USS (or enlarged). No anaemia (acutely). No renal bone disease. Previous normal Cr. Rapid onset.

CKD — Chronic Kidney Disease

Abnormalities of kidney structure or function present for >3 months

Small shrunken kidneys on USS (except DM, ADPKD, amyloid — normal/large). Anaemia (↓EPO). Renal bone disease. Elevated PTH. Long history.

AKI KDIGO staging
StageCreatinine criteriaUrine output criteria
AKI 11.5–1.9× baseline OR ↑≥26 µmol/L<0.5 mL/kg/hr for 6–12h
AKI 22.0–2.9× baseline<0.5 mL/kg/hr for ≥12h
AKI 3≥3× baseline OR Cr ≥354 µmol/L OR RRT<0.3 mL/kg/hr for ≥24h OR anuria ≥12h
03
AKI Classification — Pre-renal, Intrinsic, Post-renal
The urea:creatinine ratio and urine studies guide you
▾
FeaturePre-renalIntrinsic (ATN)Post-renal
Urea:Cr ratio>100:1~50:1 (normal)Variable
Urine Na⁺<20 mmol/L>40 mmol/LVariable
FENa<1%>2%Variable
Urine osmolality>500 mOsm/kg<350 mOsm/kgVariable
Urine sedimentNormal / hyaline castsMuddy brown casts (granular)Normal
Response to fluidsRapid improvementNo improvementImproves after relief
CausesHypovolaemia, low CO, NSAIDs, ACEi, sepsisIschaemia, gentamicin, contrast, myoglobin, myelomaBPH, stones, pelvic mass, papillary necrosis
FENa = (urine Na × plasma Cr) ÷ (plasma Na × urine Cr) × 100. FENa <1% in pre-renal (kidneys avidly retaining Na). Unreliable if on diuretics → use FEUrea instead (<35% = pre-renal).
04
Electrolytes — Na⁺, K⁺, and their disorders
Life-threatening electrolyte emergencies you must recognise
▾
Hyponatraemia (Na⁺ <135) — classify by osmolality + volume
TypeSerum OsmUrine NaCauses
Hypervolaemic hyponatraemiaLow<20Heart failure, cirrhosis, nephrotic syndrome — excess water retention
Euvolaemic hyponatraemiaLow>20SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency, reset osmostat
Hypovolaemic hyponatraemiaLow<20 (renal Na loss >20)GI losses, burns. If urine Na >20: Addison's, diuretics, RTA
PseudohyponatraemiaNormal/High—Hyperglycaemia, hyperlipidaemia, hyperproteinaemia
🚨 Correction of hyponatraemia: Max 8–10 mmol/L per 24h (chronic). Overcorrection → osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS / central pontine myelinolysis). Irreversible. Devastating neurological injury.
Hyperkalaemia — the dangerous one
  • 5.5–6.0 — Mild: Peaked T waves on ECG
  • 6.0–6.5 — Moderate: Prolonged PR, wide QRS
  • >6.5 — Severe: Sine wave, VF, asystole → Calcium gluconate IV immediately!
05
Proteinuria & Haematuria — Kidney Damage Markers
Structural damage markers — essential for CKD risk stratification
▾
Proteinuria grading (ACR = albumin:creatinine ratio)
ACR (mg/mmol)CategorySignificance
<3A1 — NormalNo significant proteinuria
3–30A2 — Moderately increasedMicroalbuminuria. Early DM nephropathy. Start ACEi/ARB
>30A3 — Severely increasedMacroalbuminuria. Nephrotic range if >300 mg/mmol. Nephrology referral
Nephrotic vs Nephritic syndrome

Nephrotic Syndrome

Proteinuria >3.5g/24h (or PCR >300) + Hypoalbuminaemia + Oedema ± Hyperlipidaemia

Causes: MCD (children), FSGS (adults), membranous (middle-aged, anti-PLA2R), diabetic nephropathy, amyloid

Nephritic Syndrome

Haematuria + Proteinuria (sub-nephrotic) + Hypertension + Oliguria + AKI

Causes: IgA nephropathy, post-strep GN, ANCA vasculitis, anti-GBM (Goodpasture's), lupus nephritis, MPGN

06
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
RFT critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ RFTs — Quick Reference

Hyperkalaemia >6.5Calcium gluconate IV first. ECG changes = emergency. Avoid K⁺-sparing drugs
AKI Stage 3Cr ≥3× baseline. Stop nephrotoxins. Fluid challenge if pre-renal. Urology if obstruction
HyponatraemiaCorrect max 8 mmol/L/24h. Overcorrection = ODS/CPM. Check volume status first
Pulmonary Oedema + AKIUraemic pulmonary oedema. Urgent dialysis may be needed. Loop diuretics first
eGFR <15Kidney failure. Prepare for RRT. Transplant assessment. AV fistula
Obstruction (Post-renal AKI)USS bladder + kidneys urgently. Catheterise / nephrostomy. Relieve then watch for post-obstructive diuresis
RhabdomyolysisAKI + very high Cr/urea + CK >1000. Myoglobinuria. Aggressive IV fluids (target UO 200–300 mL/hr)
Contrast NephropathyAKI 24–72h post-contrast. Pre-hydrate. Avoid nephrotoxins. Hold metformin
🦋
Thyroid Function Tests (TFTs)
Systematic interpretation: TSH first, then FT4/FT3, then clinical context + antibodies
TSH
0.4 – 4.0 mIU/L
Free T4 (FT4)
9 – 25 pmol/L
Free T3 (FT3)
3.5 – 7.8 pmol/L
TPO antibodies
<35 IU/mL
TRAb
Negative
Thyroglobulin Ab
<40 IU/mL
01
The TSH — Your Primary Screen
TSH is the most sensitive test for thyroid dysfunction — always start here
▾
TSH low (<0.4)
Thyroid hormone excess (hyperthyroidism) or excess exogenous T4. Check FT4 + FT3 next
TSH normal (0.4–4.0)
Euthyroid. TSH is a log-linear scale — small changes are significant. If TSH normal + symptoms = unlikely thyroid cause
TSH high (>4.0)
Hypothyroidism (primary) or TSH-secreting pituitary adenoma (rare). Check FT4 next
TSH interpretation nuances
  • ⚠️TSH lag: TSH takes 4–6 weeks to reflect changes in thyroid hormone. Don't recheck TFTs too early after starting/adjusting treatment!
  • 🏥Sick euthyroid (non-thyroidal illness): In acute illness, TSH + T3 + T4 all drop non-specifically. Do NOT treat unless truly hypothyroid
  • 🤰Pregnancy: TSH naturally lower (hCG cross-reacts with TSH receptor). Upper limit of normal trimester 1 = ~2.5 mIU/L
  • 💊Drugs that suppress TSH: Steroids, dopamine, metformin, amiodarone, somatostatin analogues
02
Pattern Recognition — The TFT Matrix
TSH + FT4 + FT3 combinations and what they mean
▾
TSHFT4FT3PatternDiagnosis
↓↑↑Overt hyperthyroidismGraves', toxic MNG, toxic adenoma, thyroiditis (early), factitious
↓Normal↑T3 toxicosisEarly Graves', toxic adenoma — FT3 rises first
↓NormalNormalSubclinical hyperthyroidismSame causes. Risk of AF, osteoporosis. Treat if TSH <0.1
↑↓Low/normalOvert hypothyroidismHashimoto's, post-ablation, post-thyroidectomy, iodine deficiency
↑NormalNormalSubclinical hypothyroidismEarly Hashimoto's. Treat if TSH >10 or symptomatic or pregnant
↓↓↓Central hypothyroidismPituitary/hypothalamic disease. Sheehan's, craniopharyngioma, pituitary adenoma
↑↑—TSH-secreting adenomaRare pituitary tumour. Check MRI pituitary
↓/Normal↓/Normal↓Sick euthyroidAcute illness. Do NOT treat. Recheck after recovery
03
Hyperthyroidism — Causes & Clinical
Not all hyperthyroidism is Graves' — differentiate carefully
▾
Causes of hyperthyroidism
CauseKey FeaturesAntibodiesUptake scan
Graves' diseaseYoung woman, goitre, exophthalmos, pretibial myxoedema, thyroid bruitTRAb +ve (80–95%)Diffuse increased uptake
Toxic MNGOlder patient, multinodular goitre, no eye signsNegativePatchy increased uptake
Toxic adenomaSingle nodule, younger patientNegativeHot nodule + suppressed rest
Thyroiditis (De Quervain's)Painful goitre, post-viral, high CRP/ESR, self-limitingTPO may be +veReduced uptake
Amiodarone-inducedType 1: iodine excess (MNG). Type 2: destructive thyroiditis. Complex managementVariableVariable
FactitiousExogenous T4 ingestion. Low thyroglobulin!NegativeSuppressed
🚨 Thyroid storm: Extreme hyperthyroidism with multi-organ failure. Burch-Wartofsky score >45 = probable storm. Treat with PTU + beta-blockers + iodine (Lugol's) + steroids + supportive. ICU admission.
04
Hypothyroidism — Causes, Features & Treatment
The great masquerader — think of it in every vague presentation
▾
Causes of hypothyroidism
CauseKey FeaturesAntibodies
Hashimoto's thyroiditisMost common in developed world. Women. Goitre → atrophic. TPO Ab +veTPO Ab (95%), TgAb
Post-ablation / thyroidectomyHistory of treatment for hyperthyroidism or thyroid cancer—
Iodine deficiencyDeveloping world. Endemic goitre. Cretinism if maternal deficiencyNegative
Drug-inducedAmiodarone (iodine content), lithium, interferon-α, tyrosine kinase inhibitorsVariable
Central (secondary)Pituitary/hypothalamic disease. TSH is low despite low FT4. Check other pituitary axesNegative
CongenitalNewborn screening programme. Cretinism if untreated — irreversible intellectual disability—
Features of hypothyroidism — "SLOW"

Symptoms

Fatigue, weight gain, cold intolerance, constipation, depression, menorrhagia, hair loss, dry skin, hoarse voice, carpal tunnel, bradycardia, delayed tendon reflexes, myxoedematous facies

Treatment

Levothyroxine (T4). Start 50 mcg daily (25 mcg if elderly/cardiac disease). Titrate 6-weekly based on TSH. Target TSH 0.5–2.5 in pregnancy. Take 30–60 min before food.

🚨 Myxoedema coma: Hypothermia + hypoglycaemia + bradycardia + altered consciousness + hyponatraemia. ICU. IV T4 ± T3. Hydrocortisone (may have concurrent adrenal insufficiency). Avoid rapid warming.
05
Thyroid Antibodies & Special Scenarios
When to check antibodies and what they mean
▾
Thyroid antibodies guide
AntibodyPositive inClinical significance
TPO Ab (anti-thyroid peroxidase)Hashimoto's (95%), Graves' (70%), 10% normal populationAutoimmune thyroid disease marker. Risk of developing hypothyroidism. Check in subclinical hypothyroidism
TRAb (TSH receptor Ab)Graves' disease (80–95%)Diagnostic of Graves'. Predicts relapse risk after antithyroid drugs. Neonatal Graves' if high in pregnancy
Thyroglobulin Ab (TgAb)Hashimoto's, Graves'Interferes with thyroglobulin assay (used in thyroid cancer follow-up). Check TgAb whenever checking Tg
Amiodarone and thyroid
  • 💊Amiodarone contains 37% iodine by weight. Check TFTs before starting and every 6 months
  • 1️⃣Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis type 1: Excess iodine → excess hormone synthesis (iodine-induced). Underlying thyroid disease. Treat with thionamides + potassium perchlorate
  • 2️⃣Amiodarone-induced thyrotoxicosis type 2: Destructive thyroiditis → leakage of hormone. Normal thyroid. Treat with prednisolone
  • ❄️Amiodarone-induced hypothyroidism: More common. Iodine excess inhibits synthesis (Wolff-Chaikoff). Treat with levothyroxine
06
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
TFT critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ TFTs — Quick Reference

Thyroid StormFever + tachyarrhythmia + confusion. Burch-Wartofsky >45. ICU. PTU + propranolol + Lugol's + steroids
Myxoedema ComaHypothermia + bradycardia + ↓GCS. ICU. IV T4 ± T3. Hydrocortisone. Slow warming
Graves' diseaseTSH↓↓, FT4↑, FT3↑, TRAb +ve, exophthalmos. Carbimazole or PTU
De Quervain'sPainful goitre, post-viral, raised ESR/CRP, self-limiting. NSAIDs ± steroids
Subclinical HypoTSH >10 = treat. TSH 4–10 = treat if symptomatic, TPO +ve, or pregnant
Amiodarone toxicityCheck TFTs before and 6-monthly. Type 1 vs Type 2 = different treatment
Sick EuthyroidLow T3 + T4 in acute illness. TSH usually normal or low. Do NOT treat. Repeat after recovery
Central HypothyroidismLow FT4 + low/normal TSH. Check other pituitary axes. MRI pituitary
💨
Pulmonary Function Tests (PFTs)
Spirometry, lung volumes, DLCO — obstructive vs restrictive vs mixed pattern interpretation
FVC
>80% predicted
FEV₁
>80% predicted
FEV₁/FVC
>0.70 (70%)
TLC
80–120% predicted
RV
80–120% predicted
DLCO
>75% predicted
RV/TLC ratio
<0.40
Peak Flow
Varies by age/sex/height
01
Is the Spirometry Acceptable Quality?
Garbage in = garbage out. Always check test quality first
▾
ATS/ERS acceptability criteria
  • 📈Reproducibility: ≥3 acceptable efforts. Best FVC and FEV₁ within 150 mL of each other
  • ⚡Effort: Rapid, forceful start. Peak flow should be achieved within 0.1s (steep initial upstroke)
  • ⏱️Duration: Exhalation ≥6 seconds (≥3 seconds in children) OR plateau on volume-time curve
  • 🔄No artefacts: No cough in first second, no glottic closure, no variable effort
⚠️ A poorly performed spirometry is worse than no spirometry — a patient straining suboptimally can mimic obstruction. Report quality in your interpretation.
02
Step 2 — Obstructive vs Restrictive vs Mixed
The FEV₁/FVC ratio is the key discriminator
▾
Pattern identification algorithm
PatternFEV₁/FVCFVCFEV₁TLC
Normal>0.70NormalNormalNormal
Obstructive<0.70Normal or ↓↓↓Normal or ↑ (air trapping)
Restrictive>0.70 (normal or ↑)↓ <80%↓ (proportional)↓ <80% (confirms restriction)
Mixed<0.70↓↓↓Variable
💡 FVC alone cannot diagnose restriction — low FVC can occur in obstruction (air trapping) or poor effort. TLC is the gold standard for restriction. FVC <80% + normal FEV₁/FVC = possible restriction. Always confirm with lung volumes (plethysmography or gas dilution).
Severity grading of obstruction (FEV₁ % predicted)
Mild
FEV₁ ≥70%
Moderate
FEV₁ 50–69%
Mod-severe
FEV₁ 30–49%
Severe
FEV₁ <30%
03
Obstructive Pattern — Causes & COPD Staging
FEV₁/FVC <0.70 — narrow the differential
▾
Obstructive pattern causes

Reversible Obstruction

  • Asthma — FEV₁ improves ≥12% AND 200mL post-bronchodilator (significant reversibility)
  • Allergic bronchopulmonary aspergillosis (ABPA)

Irreversible / Fixed Obstruction

  • COPD (smoking, α1-antitrypsin deficiency)
  • Bronchiectasis
  • Obliterative bronchiolitis
  • Lymphangioleiomyomatosis (LAM)
COPD severity — GOLD staging (FEV₁ post-bronchodilator)
GOLD StageFEV₁ % predictedSymptoms
GOLD 1 — Mild≥80%Often no symptoms. Spirometry finding only
GOLD 2 — Moderate50–79%Exertional dyspnoea. Start LABA/LAMA
GOLD 3 — Severe30–49%Marked dyspnoea. Frequent exacerbations. Add ICS
GOLD 4 — Very severe<30%Severe disability. LTOT criteria. Palliative discussion
💡 Bronchodilator reversibility testing: Give salbutamol 400 mcg via spacer, repeat spirometry at 15–20 min. Significant = FEV₁ ↑≥12% AND ≥200mL → suggests asthma over COPD (though overlap exists).
04
Restrictive Pattern — Causes & Lung Volumes
FEV₁/FVC normal + TLC <80% = true restriction
▾
Restrictive pattern causes

Intrinsic (Parenchymal)

  • Pulmonary fibrosis (IPF, UIP)
  • Sarcoidosis (stages 2–4)
  • Hypersensitivity pneumonitis
  • Drug-induced (amiodarone, methotrexate, bleomycin)
  • Connective tissue disease (SLE, RA, SSc)

Extrinsic (Chest Wall / Pleural)

  • Kyphoscoliosis
  • Obesity hypoventilation
  • Pleural effusion / fibrosis
  • Ankylosing spondylitis
  • Pectus excavatum

Neuromuscular

  • Motor neurone disease
  • Myasthenia gravis
  • Duchenne muscular dystrophy
  • Diaphragm paralysis
  • Guillain-Barré (acute)
Key: upright vs supine FVC
💡 In neuromuscular disease, FVC drops significantly (≥20%) when measured supine vs upright — indicates diaphragm weakness. Test this in MND, GBS, myasthenia! Fall in supine FVC = early sign of nocturnal hypoventilation → needs NIV.
05
DLCO — Diffusion Capacity
Gas transfer across the alveolar-capillary membrane
▾
DLCO interpretation
DLCOWith obstructionWith restrictionIsolated
Low DLCOEmphysema (alveolar destruction), obliterative bronchiolitisIPF, asbestosis, sarcoidosis, drug-induced fibrosisPulmonary hypertension, PE (acute/chronic), anaemia, early ILD
Normal/High DLCOAsthma (usually normal), chronic bronchitisChest wall / neuromuscular restriction (normal gas exchange)Polycythaemia, pulmonary haemorrhage (Hb in airspaces binds CO!)
DLCO correction
  • 🩸Always correct DLCO for haemoglobin — anaemia falsely lowers it. DLCO correction = DLCO × (10.22 + Hb) ÷ (1.7 × Hb)
  • 🌡️DLCO high + obstruction = ASTHMA (not COPD/emphysema). Emphysema always reduces DLCO
  • 💉Pulmonary haemorrhage (Goodpasture's, vasculitis) = paradoxically ELEVATED DLCO — haemoglobin in alveolar spaces binds CO
06
Flow-Volume Loops & Special Patterns
The shape of the loop tells a story
▾
Flow-volume loop patterns
PatternShapeCause
NormalTriangular expiratory limb, smooth inspiratory limb—
ObstructiveScooped-out / concave expiratory limb (classic "excavation")COPD, asthma, bronchiectasis
RestrictiveSmall loop, tall narrow shape, normal contourFibrosis, chest wall disease
Fixed upper airway obstructionFlattening of BOTH inspiratory AND expiratory limbsTracheal stenosis, goitre compressing trachea, subglottic stenosis
Variable extrathoracic obstructionFlattening of inspiratory limb onlyVocal cord dysfunction, unilateral cord palsy, subglottic lesion
Variable intrathoracic obstructionFlattening of expiratory limb onlyTracheomalacia, intrathoracic tracheal tumour
⚠️ Flattening of both expiratory AND inspiratory limbs = fixed large airway obstruction. Think tracheal stenosis, goitre, post-intubation stenosis. CT trachea + ENT/thoracic surgery referral urgently.
07
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
PFT critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ PFTs — Quick Reference

FEV₁ <1L or <30%Very severe obstruction. LTOT criteria if hypoxic. Palliative discussion. Lung transplant?
Asthma vs COPDAsthma: DLCO normal/↑, reversibility ≥12%+200mL. COPD: DLCO↓, poor/no reversibility, smoker
IPF patternRestriction + DLCO↓ + bibasal Velcro crackles + UIP on HRCT. Antifibrotics (nintedanib/pirfenidone)
α1-Antitrypsin DeficiencyEarly-onset emphysema, non-smoker or minimal smoking, lower lobe predominance
Pulmonary HypertensionIsolated DLCO↓ with normal spirometry. Echocardiogram + RHC for confirmation
Fixed airway obstructionFlat loop both limbs. Tracheal stenosis / goitre. CT trachea urgently
MND / GBSRestrictive + FVC drop >20% supine. Diaphragm weakness. NIV when FVC <50%
Pulmonary HaemorrhageElevated DLCO + haemoptysis. Goodpasture's / ANCA vasculitis. Anti-GBM antibodies
🩸
Complete Blood Count (CBC / FBC)
Systematic: RBC/anaemia → WBC → platelets → blood film — diagnosis by indices and morphology
Hb (M/F)
130–180 / 120–160 g/L
MCV
80–100 fL
MCH
27–33 pg
MCHC
315–360 g/L
WBC
4–11 × 10⁹/L
Neutrophils
1.8–7.5 × 10⁹/L
Lymphocytes
1.0–4.0 × 10⁹/L
Platelets
150–400 × 10⁹/L
Reticulocytes
25–100 × 10⁹/L
Eosinophils
0.04–0.4 × 10⁹/L
01
Anaemia — Classify by MCV First
MCV-based classification is the most systematic approach
▾
WHO anaemia definition: Hb <130 g/L (men), <120 g/L (women), <110 g/L (pregnant)

Microcytic (MCV <80 fL)

TAILS:

  • Thalassaemia
  • Anaemia of chronic disease (can be)
  • Iron deficiency anaemia
  • Lead poisoning
  • Sideroblastic anaemia

Most common = IDA

Normocytic (MCV 80–100 fL)

  • Anaemia of chronic disease (most common)
  • Acute blood loss
  • Haemolysis (early)
  • Aplastic anaemia
  • Chronic kidney disease (↓EPO)
  • Mixed deficiency (B12 + iron)
  • Hypothyroidism

Macrocytic (MCV >100 fL)

ABCDE:

  • Alcohol / Liver disease
  • B12 / Folate deficiency
  • Cytotoxic drugs (methotrexate, hydroxyurea, AZT)
  • Dysplastic (MDS)
  • Endocrine (hypothyroidism)
  • Reticulocytosis (haemolysis, haemorrhage)
02
Iron Deficiency vs Anaemia of Chronic Disease
The two most common anaemias — differentiate them precisely
▾
ParameterIron Deficiency AnaemiaAnaemia of Chronic DiseaseCombined
MCV↓ microcyticNormal or ↓Often normal
Serum Iron↓ Low↓ Low↓ Low
TIBC / Transferrin↑ High↓ Low/normalNormal
Transferrin Saturation↓ <16%↓ <16%↓
Ferritin↓ <12 µg/L↑ Normal or elevatedVariable
Soluble TfR↑ ElevatedNormal↑
Reticulocyte Hb↓ <28 pg↓↓
Blood filmHypochromic, microcytic, pencil cells, target cellsNormochromic normocytic (usually)Mixed
⚠️ Ferritin is an acute-phase reactant — can be falsely elevated in infection, inflammation, malignancy, liver disease. A ferritin 12–100 µg/L in a patient with chronic disease may still represent iron deficiency. Use transferrin saturation + clinical context.
03
Macrocytic Anaemia — B12 & Folate
Megaloblastic vs non-megaloblastic — the film is the key
▾
Megaloblastic anaemia (B12 or folate deficiency)
  • 🔬Blood film: Hypersegmented neutrophils (≥5 lobes in >5% neutrophils OR any neutrophil with ≥6 lobes) + macro-ovalocytes
  • 📉Pancytopenia may develop in severe cases (all cell lines affected)
  • 🔺LDH↑ + bilirubin↑ + haptoglobin↓ (intramedullary haemolysis — ineffective erythropoiesis)
B12 deficiency vs Folate deficiency
FeatureB12 DeficiencyFolate Deficiency
Neurological signsYes — SACD (subacute combined degeneration)No neurological signs
CausesPernicious anaemia (anti-IF Ab), vegan diet, gastric surgery, metformin, Crohn's (terminal ileum)Poor diet (alcohol, elderly), pregnancy, malabsorption, methotrexate, phenytoin, haemolytic anaemia (↑demand)
MMA level↑ Methylmalonic acid elevatedNormal MMA
Homocysteine↑ Elevated↑ Elevated (both raise homocysteine)
TreatmentIM B12 (hydroxocobalamin) 1 mg alt days × 2 weeks, then 3-monthlyFolic acid 5 mg/day. Correct B12 first if both deficient!
🚨 Never give folic acid alone if B12 deficiency coexists — will precipitate or worsen subacute combined degeneration of the cord (SACD). Always check B12 level before starting folate.
04
Haemolytic Anaemia
Raised LDH + low haptoglobin + reticulocytosis = haemolysis until proven otherwise
▾
Screen for haemolysis
LDH
↑ Elevated (cell lysis)
Haptoglobin
↓ Low (binds free Hb)
Bilirubin (unconjugated)
↑ Elevated (Hb breakdown)
Reticulocytes
↑ Elevated (BM response)
Intravascular vs Extravascular haemolysis

Intravascular Haemolysis

Haemoglobinaemia, haemoglobinuria (pink/red urine), haemosiderinuria. Haptoglobin very low.

Causes: TTP, HUS, MAHA, PNH, G6PD (acute), mechanical heart valves, transfusion reaction, malaria

Extravascular Haemolysis

RBCs destroyed in spleen/liver. Jaundice. Splenomegaly. Urine normal colour.

Causes: Autoimmune haemolytic anaemia (AIHA — warm or cold), hereditary spherocytosis, thalassaemia, sickle cell, hypersplenism

💡 Direct Antiglobulin Test (DAT / Coombs test): Positive = immune-mediated haemolysis (AIHA, drug-induced, transfusion reaction). Negative = non-immune haemolysis (TTP, HUS, G6PD, hereditary spherocytosis, PNH).
05
White Blood Cells — Differential Count
Each cell type elevation or reduction has a specific differential
▾
Neutrophils
Lymphocytes
Eosinophils
Monocytes
Basophils
Neutrophilia (>7.5 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🦠Bacterial infection (most common), tissue necrosis, burns, MI, surgery
  • 💊Steroids (demargination), G-CSF, lithium, adrenaline, smoking
  • 🩸Myeloproliferative neoplasms (CML — especially if >50 × 10⁹/L + left shift + basophilia)
  • 😤Physiological: exercise, pregnancy, stress, neonatal period
Neutropenia (<1.8 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🔴Neutropenia <0.5 × 10⁹/L = agranulocytosis → high risk sepsis. Neutropenic sepsis protocol!
  • 🦠Viral infections (EBV, CMV, HIV, hepatitis), sepsis (consumption)
  • 💊Drugs: carbimazole (0.5%), clozapine (1–2%), NSAIDs, sulphonamides, chemotherapy
  • 🩸Aplastic anaemia, SLE (anti-neutrophil Ab), hypersplenism, B12/folate deficiency
Lymphocytosis (>4.0 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🦠Viral infections: EBV (glandular fever), CMV, hepatitis, COVID-19, HIV (acute), pertussis ("lymphocytosis of pertussis")
  • 🩸CLL (most common leukaemia in adults — sustained lymphocytosis, smear cells on film)
  • ⚠️Absolute lymphocyte count >10 × 10⁹/L + sustained = almost always CLL or lymphoma. Flow cytometry urgently
Lymphopenia (<1.0 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🔴HIV/AIDS (CD4 count guide). Steroids/immunosuppressants. Sepsis. SLE. Sarcoidosis. Chemotherapy
  • ⚠️CD4 <200/µL = AIDS-defining. PCP, toxoplasma, CMV retinitis prophylaxis required
Eosinophilia (>0.4 × 10⁹/L)
DegreeCountCommon causes
Mild0.4–1.5Atopy (asthma, eczema, rhinitis), drug reactions, early helminth infection
Moderate1.5–5.0Parasitic infections (Toxocara, Ascaris, Strongyloides, Trichinella), drug hypersensitivity, EGPA (Churg-Strauss)
Severe / HES>5.0Hypereosinophilic syndrome (HES) → end-organ damage (cardiac, neurological). Malignancy (T-cell lymphoma, AML M4eo). Loeffler's
Monocytosis (>0.8 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🦠Chronic infections: TB (classic!), brucellosis, subacute bacterial endocarditis, syphilis
  • 🔥Inflammatory: IBD, sarcoidosis, connective tissue disease
  • 🩸CMML (chronic myelomonocytic leukaemia) — absolute monocytosis >1.0 × 10⁹/L persistently → bone marrow biopsy
  • 🔄Recovery phase after neutropenia (chemotherapy, viral illness) — monocytes recover first
Basophilia (>0.1 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🩸CML (chronic myeloid leukaemia) — basophilia is a hallmark! Also raised in PV, ET, myelofibrosis
  • 🦠Viral infections (chickenpox, influenza), hypothyroidism, IgE-mediated allergy, iron deficiency
  • ⚠️Basophils >10% of differential in high WBC context = blast crisis of CML → urgent haematology
06
Platelets — Thrombocytopenia & Thrombocytosis
Count + function + clinical context = the full picture
▾
Thrombocytopenia severity
Mild (100–150)
Usually safe. Monitor. Investigate cause
Moderate (50–100)
Avoid major surgery. Increased bleeding risk
Severe (<50)
Major bleeding risk. Transfuse if <10 or <20 with bleeding/procedure
Causes of thrombocytopenia
MechanismCauses
Decreased productionAplastic anaemia, chemotherapy, leukaemia (bone marrow replacement), B12/folate deficiency, alcohol, viral (HIV, EBV, CMV)
Increased destruction (immune)ITP (most common isolated thrombocytopenia), SLE, antiphospholipid syndrome, drug-induced (heparin = HIT, quinine, vancomycin), post-transfusion purpura
Increased destruction (non-immune)TTP (Hb↓ + plt↓ + renal failure + neuro signs + fever = pentad), HUS (E. coli O157), DIC (PT↑ + APTT↑ + fibrinogen↓ + D-dimer↑), HELLP, hypersplenism
DilutionalMassive transfusion, IV fluid resuscitation
PseudothrombocytopeniaEDTA-dependent platelet clumping. Always check blood film! Repeat in citrate tube
🚨 TTP = haematological emergency. Pentad: microangiopathic haemolytic anaemia + thrombocytopenia + renal impairment + neurological symptoms + fever. Do NOT delay plasma exchange (PEX). ADAMTS13 activity <10% confirms. Avoid platelet transfusion — "fuel to the fire."
Thrombocytosis (>450 × 10⁹/L)
  • 🔥Reactive (most common): Infection, inflammation, iron deficiency, post-splenectomy, post-surgery/trauma. CRP also elevated
  • 🩸Primary (clonal): Essential thrombocythaemia (ET), PV, CML, myelofibrosis. Persistent >600 × 10⁹/L without cause → JAK2 V617F mutation + haematology referral
07
Blood Film — The Most Informative Single Test
Always request a film when the CBC is abnormal or diagnosis is unclear
▾
RBC morphology
Cell typeAppearanceAssociations
SpherocytesSmall, dense, no central pallorHereditary spherocytosis, AIHA, burns
Schistocytes / FragmentsFragmented RBCsTTP, HUS, DIC, mechanical valve haemolysis — MAHA
Target cellsCentral dense dot + peripheral ringThalassaemia, IDA, liver disease, post-splenectomy, HbC disease
Sickle cellsCrescent-shapedSickle cell disease (HbSS)
Pencil cellsElongated, thinIron deficiency anaemia
Tear-drop cells (Dacrocytes)Tear-drop shapeMyelofibrosis, thalassaemia, marrow infiltration
RouleauxStacked coinsMultiple myeloma, inflammatory states, hyperproteinaemia
Acanthocytes (Spur cells)Spiculated, irregularLiver disease, abetalipoproteinaemia, post-splenectomy
Hypochromic microcyticSmall, paleIron deficiency, thalassaemia, lead poisoning
Howell-Jolly bodiesDense nuclear remnantsAsplenia (functional or surgical)
WBC abnormalities on film
  • 🔬Blast cells — any blasts = leukaemia until proven otherwise. Urgent haematology
  • 🔬Smear cells (Smudge cells) — CLL. Fragile lymphocytes smeared during slide preparation
  • 🔬Hypersegmented neutrophils — megaloblastic anaemia (B12/folate deficiency)
  • 🔬Left shift (band forms) — severe bacterial infection, CML, leukaemoid reaction
  • 🔬Toxic granulation + Döhle bodies — severe bacterial infection, sepsis
08
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
CBC critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ CBC — Emergency Quick Reference

TTPPlt↓ + MAHA + neuro + renal + fever. Plasma exchange NOW. Do NOT give platelets
DICPlt↓ + PT↑ + APTT↑ + fibrinogen↓ + D-dimer↑. Treat underlying cause. FFP + cryo + platelets
Febrile NeutropeniaTemp >38°C + neutrophils <0.5. Broad-spectrum antibiotics within 1 hour. Don't wait for cultures
HITPlatelets ↓>50% 5–10d post-heparin. Stop ALL heparin. Start argatroban/fondaparinux. 4T score
Blast cells on filmAny blasts = acute leukaemia until proven otherwise. Urgent haematology. NEVER delay
Pernicious AnaemiaMacrocytic + neuro signs + anti-IF antibodies. IM B12 first — then folate if needed
CLLSustained lymphocytosis + smear cells. Flow cytometry. CD5+/CD23+/CD19+ = CLL
Sickle Cell CrisisHb↓ + pain + infarction. Oxygen + IV fluids + analgesia + exchange transfusion if severe
🫁
Chest X-Ray Interpretation
Systematic ABCDE approach — never miss a finding
CTR
<0.5 (PA film)
Trachea
Midline ± 1 cm
Carina angle
<70°
Costophrenic angles
Acute / sharp
Right hemidiaphragm
Higher than left
Aortic knuckle
Visible, not >4 cm
01
Technical Adequacy — Before You Read Anything
A poor-quality film can hide or mimic pathology
▾
RIPE mnemonic for technical quality
R — Rotation
Medial clavicle ends equidistant from spinous processes. Rotation causes apparent mediastinal shift.
I — Inspiration
6 anterior ribs or 10 posterior ribs above diaphragm. Poor inspiration → apparent cardiomegaly, basal atelectasis.
P — Projection
PA (preferred): scapulae projected laterally. AP: heart appears larger, used for portable/ICU films.
E — Exposure
Correct: vertebrae visible behind heart. Under-exposed → white-out. Over-exposed → missed interstitial markings.
⚠️ Always note if AP or PA. The cardiothoracic ratio (CTR) is only valid on a PA film. Never diagnose cardiomegaly on AP alone.
02
Airway & Mediastinum
Trachea, carina, mediastinal contours — look for shift and widening
▾
Trachea
  • 📏Midline or slightly right of centre at carina. Deviation = mass, collapse, tension pneumothorax (pushes away), collapse (pulls toward)
  • 📐Carina angle <70° — widening suggests left atrial enlargement
Mediastinum
Superior mediastinum
>8 cm wide → widened. Consider aortic dissection, lymphoma, substernal goitre, thymoma. AP film widens normally.
Aortic knuckle
Loss = aortic dissection or periaortic haematoma. Prominent = hypertension, aortic aneurysm.
Right heart border
Right atrium. Bulging → RA enlargement, pericardial effusion.
Left heart border
Aortic knuckle (top) → pulmonary trunk → left atrial appendage → left ventricle (bottom).
🚨 Widened mediastinum (>8cm) + tearing chest pain = aortic dissection until proven otherwise. Do NOT give thrombolytics. Urgent CT aortogram.
03
Cardiac Silhouette
Size, shape, borders — assess every contour
▾
Cardiothoracic ratio (CTR)
CTRSignificance
<0.5Normal (PA film)
0.5–0.55Borderline — repeat if clinical concern
>0.55Cardiomegaly — LV failure, cardiomyopathy, pericardial effusion
Cardiac chamber enlargement patterns
LV enlargement
Apex displaced downward and laterally. Boot-shaped heart (cor bovinum in severe cases).
LA enlargement
Double right heart border, left atrial appendage bulge, splaying of carina (>70°), posterior displacement of oesophagus on lateral.
RV enlargement
Cardiac apex lifted off diaphragm (on lateral: retrosternal contact >⅓ of sternum).
Pericardial effusion
Globular "water bottle" heart. Rapid enlargement without pulmonary oedema. Echo to confirm.
04
Lung Fields — Zones, Densities & Key Patterns
Compare zones symmetrically; silhouette sign localises pathology
▾
Densities (most → least dense)
🤍 Metal
Clips, pacemakers, prostheses — bright white
🩶 Bone
Ribs, vertebrae, clavicles
🩶 Soft tissue / fluid
Heart, liver, consolidation, effusion
🖤 Fat
Epicardial fat pad, lipoma
🖤 Air
Lung, trachea, bowel — black
Key opacity patterns
PatternDescriptionCauses
ConsolidationAirspace opacity with air bronchogramPneumonia, pulmonary oedema, haemorrhage, infarction
InterstitialReticular, nodular, or reticulo-nodular shadowingPulmonary fibrosis, sarcoidosis, lymphangitis carcinomatosa, viral pneumonia
AtelectasisVolume loss, linear/plate/lobar collapseMucus plugging, endobronchial tumour, post-surgical
Pleural effusionBlunting of costophrenic angle, meniscus sign, dense whiteCCF (bilateral), parapneumonic, malignancy, empyema, trauma
PneumothoraxVisible lung edge, absent lung markings beyondSpontaneous, trauma, iatrogenic, tension
CavitationAir-containing lucency within opacityTB, abscess, Wegener's, squamous cell carcinoma, infarct
💡 Silhouette sign: Loss of a normally visible border indicates adjacent pathology. Lost right heart border = RML consolidation. Lost left heart border = lingular consolidation. Lost hemidiaphragm = lower lobe consolidation/effusion.
05
Diaphragm, Bones & Soft Tissues
Don't stop at the lungs — the whole film has information
▾
Diaphragm
  • 👆Right normally higher than left (liver). Left lower → gastric bubble visible beneath
  • ⬆️Elevated hemidiaphragm: phrenic nerve palsy, subphrenic pathology, collapse/consolidation of lower lobe, hepatomegaly/splenomegaly
  • ⬇️Flattened diaphragm: hyperinflation (COPD, severe asthma, tension PTX)
  • 💨Free sub-diaphragmatic air: perforated viscus — surgical emergency
Bones — count and assess every rib, clavicle, scapula, vertebra
  • 🦴Rib fractures: look for pneumothorax, haemothorax. Flail chest = 3+ consecutive ribs each fractured in 2 places
  • 🦴Lytic or sclerotic lesions: metastases (lytic = renal/lung/thyroid; sclerotic = prostate/breast), myeloma
  • 🦴Widened superior mediastinum + fractured first rib = major vascular injury until proven otherwise
Soft tissues & lines
  • 📍ETT tip: 3–7 cm above carina (just below T4)
  • 📍CVP line: tip in SVC/right atrium junction — not in ventricle
  • 📍NG tube: below diaphragm, left of midline (stomach)
  • 📍Chest drain: in pleural space — no kinks
06
Pulmonary Oedema — Severity Grading
Kerley lines, bat-wing shadows, effusions — grade it
▾
ABCDE of pulmonary oedema (in order of severity)
GradeFindingPCWP (approx)
1Upper lobe venous diversion (vessels in upper zones ≥ lower zones)~18 mmHg
2Kerley B lines (horizontal lines at lung periphery, interstitial oedema), haziness of hila~20–25 mmHg
3Alveolar oedema — bat-wing / butterfly perihilar opacity~25–30 mmHg
4Bilateral pleural effusions, severe widespread opacity>30 mmHg
⚠️ Unilateral pulmonary oedema may be seen after rapid drainage of a large pneumothorax (re-expansion oedema) or in the contralateral lung after prolonged lateral decubitus position. Don't always assume bilateral.
07
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
CXR critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ CXR — Emergency Quick Reference

Tension PTXAbsent breath sounds + deviated trachea (away). Don't wait for CXR. Needle decompression 2nd ICS MCL
Aortic DissectionWidened mediastinum >8 cm + tearing pain. No thrombolytics. Urgent CT aortogram
