Massive HaemothoraxOpacification + haemodynamic instability. Chest drain + urgent cardiothoracic review
Lobar CollapseTracheal/fissure deviation toward opacity. Bronchoscopy if endobronchial cause suspected
Perforated ViscusFree sub-diaphragmatic air. Erect film. Surgical emergency. CT to confirm and plan
Pulmonary OedemaBat-wing opacity + Kerley B lines + upper lobe diversion. Treat CCF urgently
Malpositioned ETTToo low → right main bronchus intubation. Pull back. Confirm 3–7 cm above carina
Cavitating LesionAlways consider TB, abscess, malignancy. Sputum cultures + AFBs + CT chest
🧪
Urinalysis Interpretation
Dipstick + microscopy — systematic approach from macroscopic to microscopic
Specific gravity
1.005–1.030
pH
4.5–8.0
Protein
Negative (trace ok)
Glucose
Negative
Ketones
Negative
Blood / Hb
Negative
Nitrite
Negative
Leukocyte esterase
Negative
Bilirubin
Negative
Urobilinogen
Normal / trace
01
Macroscopic Appearance & Specific Gravity
Look first — colour and clarity tell a story
▾
Colour interpretation
ColourCauses
Pale yellow / colourlessDilute urine — high fluid intake, diabetes insipidus
Dark yellow / amberConcentrated (dehydration, fever), normal if concentrated
OrangeRifampicin, bilirubinuria, dehydration, phenazopyridine
Red / pinkHaematuria, haemoglobinuria, myoglobinuria, beetroot, rifampicin
Brown / colaMethaemoglobinaemia, myoglobinuria (rhabdomyolysis), bilirubinuria, old blood
Milky / cloudyPyuria (UTI), chyluria, phosphaturia (normal cold urine)
Green / bluePseudomonas UTI, amitriptyline, propofol infusion
Specific gravity
SG <1.005 (Hyposthenuric)
Diabetes insipidus, psychogenic polydipsia, overhydration, early CKD (loss of concentrating ability)
SG 1.010 (Isosthenuric)
Fixed SG = CKD with loss of concentrating ability. Urine osmolality = plasma.
SG >1.020 (Hypersthenuric)
Dehydration, SIADH, post-contrast (falsely elevated), prerenal AKI, adrenal insufficiency
02
Protein & Glucose
Proteinuria grading and glucosuria interpretation
▾
Proteinuria — dipstick is semi-quantitative
DipstickApproximate proteinNext step
Trace<300 mg/LOften benign if isolated. Repeat with MSU
1+ (~0.3 g/L)~300 mg/LQuantify: urine PCR / ACR. Exclude UTI first
2+ (~1 g/L)~1000 mg/LSignificant — urgent urine PCR. Consider glomerulonephritis
3+ (~3 g/L)~3000 mg/LNephrotic range. Urine PCR >300 mg/mmol. Nephrology referral
4+ (>20 g/L)>20,000 mg/LHeavy proteinuria. Exclude Bence-Jones protein (myeloma) with immunofixation
⚠️ Dipstick detects albumin poorly for Bence-Jones protein (light chains in myeloma). Request urine protein electrophoresis / immunofixation if myeloma suspected. Dipstick can be negative despite heavy light chain proteinuria.
Glucosuria
  • 🔴Diabetes mellitus: Blood glucose >10 mmol/L exceeds renal threshold. Always check blood glucose.
  • 🟡Renal glucosuria: Normal blood glucose. Defective tubular reabsorption (Fanconi syndrome, pregnancy, SGLT2 inhibitors).
  • 🟡Gestational diabetes: Lower renal threshold in pregnancy. Check GTT.
03
Blood, Nitrite & Leucocyte Esterase
The UTI and haematuria triad
▾
Blood (haematuria)
Microscopic haematuria
>3 RBC/HPF on microscopy. Dipstick positive blood without visible colour change. Always investigate if persistent.
Macroscopic haematuria
Visible red/brown urine. Always urgent investigation regardless of age. Urgent urology referral.
Haemoglobinuria
Dipstick positive for blood, no RBCs on microscopy. Suggests haemolysis (intravascular). Check LDH, haptoglobin, blood film.
Myoglobinuria
Dipstick positive, no RBCs. Rhabdomyolysis. Check CK urgently. Risk of AKI. Aggressive IV hydration.
Nitrite & Leucocyte Esterase (UTI dipstick)
NitriteLEInterpretation
PositivePositiveHigh probability of UTI — gram-negative bacteria (E.coli, Klebsiella, Proteus) convert nitrate → nitrite
NegativePositiveUTI possible — gram-positive bacteria (Enterococcus, Staph saprophyticus) don't produce nitrite. Send MSU.
PositiveNegativeNitrite positive alone — some Enterobacteriaceae. Clinical context important. Send MSU.
NegativeNegativeUTI unlikely (NPV ~95%). Consider other causes if symptoms persist.
💡 False negative nitrite: Enterococcus, Pseudomonas, Staph saprophyticus (do not produce nitrite). Short dwell time in bladder (<4h). False positive LE: vaginal contamination, trichomonas, tissue inflammation without bacteria.
04
Ketones, Bilirubin & Urobilinogen
Metabolic clues from a simple dipstick
▾
Ketones
  • 🔴Diabetic ketoacidosis (DKA): Moderate–large ketones + hyperglycaemia + acidosis. Urgent management protocol.
  • 🟡Alcoholic ketoacidosis: Ketones + low/normal glucose. History of heavy alcohol. Normal or low blood glucose.
  • 🟡Starvation ketosis: Mild ketonuria. Normal glucose. Prolonged fasting, hyperemesis gravidarum.
  • 🟢Low-carbohydrate diet / SGLT2 inhibitors: Mild ketones. Euglycaemic DKA is a rare but serious SGLT2i complication.
Bilirubin & Urobilinogen — jaundice pattern
Urine BilirubinUrine UrobilinogenPatternCauses
NegativeNormal/↑Pre-hepatic (haemolysis)Haemolytic anaemia, sickle cell, G6PD
PositiveNormal/↑Hepatic (hepatocellular)Viral hepatitis, alcoholic hepatitis, cirrhosis
PositiveAbsent/↓Post-hepatic (obstructive)Gallstones, cholangiocarcinoma, pancreatic cancer
05
Urine Microscopy — Casts & Cells
Microscopy tells you WHERE the problem is in the nephron
▾
Urinary casts — localise renal disease
Cast typeSignificance
Hyaline castsNormal — concentrated urine, exercise, dehydration
RBC castsGlomerulonephritis — diagnostic. Suggests nephritic syndrome
WBC castsAcute interstitial nephritis (AIN), pyelonephritis
Granular casts ("muddy brown")Acute tubular necrosis (ATN) — ischaemic or toxic AKI
Waxy / broad castsAdvanced CKD — wide tubules in end-stage nephrons
Fatty casts / oval fat bodiesNephrotic syndrome — "Maltese crosses" under polarised light
Tubular epithelial cell castsATN, nephrotoxic drug injury
💡 RBC casts = glomerular origin of haematuria. Dysmorphic RBCs (acanthocytes) on phase contrast microscopy also indicate glomerular disease. Isomorphic RBCs suggest lower urinary tract bleeding.
06
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
Urinalysis critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ Urinalysis — Quick Reference

DKALarge ketones + glucose + acidosis. Urgent insulin + IV fluids. Hourly monitoring
MyoglobinuriaCola urine + CK >5000 U/L. Aggressive IVF 200–300 mL/hr. Monitor creatinine
Macroscopic HaematuriaAlways urgent urology. Rule out bladder/renal malignancy, stones, glomerulonephritis
RBC CastsGlomerulonephritis. Check ANCA, ANA, anti-GBM, complement, ASOT. Nephrology urgently
Heavy Proteinuria (3+/4+)Nephrotic syndrome: oedema + hypoalbuminaemia + hyperlipidaemia. Renal biopsy likely needed
Bence-JonesDipstick neg but high protein on Urine PE. Myeloma screen: SPEP + immunofixation + serum free light chains
Sterile PyuriaWBCs but no bacteria. Consider TB (AFB × 3 early morning urine), interstitial nephritis, partially treated UTI
Glucosuria + Normal BSLRenal glucosuria. Consider Fanconi syndrome — check phosphate, bicarbonate, uric acid, amino acids
💧
Body Fluid Analysis
Pleural, peritoneal (ascitic), CSF, synovial, pericardial — Light's criteria and beyond
01
Pleural Fluid — Light's Criteria
Transudate vs exudate — the most important first step
▾
Light's Criteria — exudate if ANY ONE of:
Protein ratio
Pleural protein / Serum protein >0.5
LDH ratio
Pleural LDH / Serum LDH >0.6
Pleural LDH
>⅔ upper limit of normal serum LDH
⚠️ Light's misclassifies ~25% of CCF transudates as exudates (especially if on diuretics). Use serum albumin − pleural albumin: if >12 g/L, it is a transudate regardless of Light's criteria.
Causes by type
TransudateExudate
CCF (most common bilateral)Parapneumonic / empyema
Hypoalbuminaemia (cirrhosis, nephrotic)Malignancy (any cancer — lung, breast, lymphoma)
HypothyroidismTB pleuritis (exudate, lymphocyte predominant)
Meig's syndrome (ovarian fibroma)PE (haemorrhagic, exudate ~80%)
Constrictive pericarditisRheumatoid / SLE pleuritis
Peritoneal dialysisChylothorax (milky, triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L)
Additional pleural fluid tests
  • 🔴pH <7.2: Empyema or complicated parapneumonic — chest drain needed
  • 🔴Glucose <2.2 mmol/L: Empyema, RA, malignancy, TB, oesophageal rupture
  • 🟡Amylase elevated: Pancreatic effusion, oesophageal rupture, malignancy
  • 🟡Cytology: Malignant cells — pleural carcinoma. Repeat ×3 for sensitivity
  • 🟢AFB / culture: TB pleuritis (culture yield ~30%). Pleural biopsy better if suspected
02
Ascitic Fluid — SAAG & Causes
Serum-ascites albumin gradient to classify ascites
▾
SAAG (Serum Albumin − Ascites Albumin)
SAAG ≥11 g/L (High)
Portal hypertension. Accuracy 97%. Cirrhosis, CCF, Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosis, right heart failure, constrictive pericarditis
SAAG <11 g/L (Low)
No portal hypertension. TB peritonitis, malignant ascites, nephrotic syndrome, pancreatitis, serositis (SLE)
SBP (Spontaneous Bacterial Peritonitis) diagnosis
🚨 Ascitic PMN count ≥250 cells/mm³ = SBP until proven otherwise. Start empirical antibiotics immediately (cefotaxime 2g TDS). Do NOT wait for culture results. Avoid aminoglycosides in cirrhosis (nephrotoxic). IV albumin (1.5 g/kg at diagnosis, 1 g/kg on day 3) reduces HRS risk.
Additional ascitic tests
  • 🔬Cell count: >500 WBC (lymphocyte predominant) → TB peritonitis (PMN if acute). Cytology for malignancy
  • 🧫Culture in blood culture bottles at bedside (30 mL): increases yield from 40% → 70%
  • 💛Amylase: elevated (>200 U/L) → pancreatic ascites
  • 🔴Haemorrhagic ascites (RBC >50,000/mm³): malignancy, trauma, hepatocellular carcinoma
03
CSF Analysis — Meningitis Pattern Recognition
Rapid interpretation can be life-saving — know the patterns
▾
Normal CSF values
Opening pressure
10–20 cmH₂O
Colour
Clear, colourless
WBC
<5 cells/mm³ (lymphocytes)
Protein
0.15–0.45 g/L
Glucose
2.5–4.5 mmol/L (60–80% serum)
CSF pattern recognition
ConditionAppearanceWBC & typeProteinGlucose (CSF/serum)
Bacterial meningitisTurbid / purulent↑↑ PMN (>1000)↑↑ >1 g/L↓↓ <0.5
Viral (aseptic) meningitisClear↑ Lymphocytes (50–500)Normal/↑ slightlyNormal (>0.6)
TB meningitisClear / xanthochromic↑ Lymphocytes (100–400)↑↑ 1–5 g/L↓↓ <0.5 (very low)
Fungal (Cryptococcus)Clear (India ink +ve)↑ Lymphocytes↑↓
Subarachnoid haemorrhageBloody → xanthochromic (>2h)RBCs, xanthochromia↑Normal
Guillain-BarréClearNormal (<5)↑↑ (cyto-albuminous dissociation)Normal
🚨 Do NOT delay antibiotics for LP if bacterial meningitis is suspected. Ceftriaxone 2g IV immediately + dexamethasone 0.15 mg/kg QDS. Contraindications to LP: raised ICP, coagulopathy, local infection. CT head first only if clinical features of raised ICP (papilloedema, focal neuro deficit, GCS <13, immunocompromised).
04
Synovial Fluid — Joint Aspiration
Classify arthritis: crystal, septic, or inflammatory
▾
Synovial fluid classification
TypeAppearanceWBC/mm³PMN %Diagnosis
NormalClear, straw-coloured<200<25%Normal
Non-inflammatoryClear to slightly turbid200–2000<25%OA, trauma, haemarthrosis
InflammatoryTurbid, yellow2000–50,000>50%RA, reactive arthritis, gout, pseudogout, SLE
SepticPurulent, opaque>50,000 (often >100,000)>75%Septic arthritis (Staph aureus most common)
HaemorrhagicBloodyVariableVariableTrauma, haemophilia, pseudogout, tumour
Crystal analysis (polarised light microscopy)
Gout (MSU crystals)
Negatively birefringent. Needle-shaped. Yellow when parallel to compensator. Monosodium urate.
Pseudogout (CPPD crystals)
Positively birefringent. Rhomboid / rod-shaped. Blue when parallel to compensator. Calcium pyrophosphate.
🚨 Septic arthritis is a surgical emergency. WBC >50,000 with >75% PMN = septic until proven otherwise. Joint washout + IV antibiotics. Delay → cartilage destruction within 24–48h. Never withhold antibiotics awaiting crystal analysis.
05
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
Body Fluid critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ Body Fluids — Quick Reference

Bacterial MeningitisTurbid CSF + PMN↑↑ + glucose↓↓. Ceftriaxone + dexamethasone immediately. Don't delay for CT unless indicated
SBPAscites PMN ≥250. Cefotaxime + IV albumin. Risk of hepatorenal syndrome
Septic ArthritisJoint WBC >50,000. Surgical washout + antibiotics. Urgent orthopaedics
EmpyemaPleural pH <7.2 + turbid fluid. Chest drain + antibiotics. May need VATS if loculated
SAHBloody LP → xanthochromia (>2h after ictus). CT first. Neurosurgery/IR referral
TB MeningitisCSF glucose very low + lymphocytes + high protein. AFB stain. 4-drug ATT + steroids
ChylothoraxMilky white pleural fluid. Triglycerides >1.24 mmol/L. Lymphoma/trauma. Low-fat diet or surgery
Gout vs PseudogoutPolarised light: needle = MSU (gout), rhomboid = CPPD (pseudogout). Treat inflammation — NSAIDs/colchicine/steroids
⚡
Electrolyte Interpretation
Sodium, potassium, calcium, magnesium, phosphate — systematic approach with ECG correlations
Sodium
135–145 mmol/L
Potassium
3.5–5.0 mmol/L
Calcium (total)
2.12–2.62 mmol/L
Corrected Ca²⁺
Measured Ca + 0.02 × (40 − albumin)
Magnesium
0.7–1.0 mmol/L
Phosphate
0.8–1.5 mmol/L
Chloride
98–106 mmol/L
01
Hyponatraemia — The Most Common Electrolyte Disorder
Assess volume status FIRST — it determines treatment
▾
Hyponatraemia definition: Na <135 mmol/L — Classify by severity
Mild (130–134)
Often asymptomatic. Nausea, malaise, headache.
Moderate (125–129)
Vomiting, confusion, drowsiness.
Severe (<125)
Seizures, coma, respiratory arrest. URGENT.
Step-by-step approach
StepTestFinding → Diagnosis
1. Exclude pseudohyponatraemiaSerum osmolalityNormal (280–295) = artefact (high lipid/protein). Low (<275) = true hyponatraemia
2. Assess volume statusExam + urine NaSee below ↓
3. HypovolaemicUrine Na<20 = extra-renal loss (vomiting, diarrhoea). >20 = renal loss (diuretics, Addison's)
4. EuvolaemicUrine osmolality>100 = SIADH, hypothyroidism, adrenal insufficiency. <100 = polydipsia, reset osmostat
5. HypervolaemicClinicalCCF (urine Na <20), cirrhosis (urine Na <20), nephrotic (urine Na <20), AKI/CKD (urine Na >20)
🚨 Osmotic demyelination syndrome (ODS / central pontine myelinolysis) occurs if hyponatraemia is corrected TOO FAST. Maximum correction: 8–10 mmol/L per 24h (some guidelines: 10–12 mmol/L). Risk factors: alcoholism, malnutrition, liver disease. If overcorrected → relower Na with desmopressin + 5% dextrose.
SIADH Diagnostic Criteria (Schwartz-Bartter)
  • ✅Plasma osmolality <275 mOsm/kg
  • ✅Urine osmolality >100 mOsm/kg (inappropriately concentrated)
  • ✅Urine sodium >30 mmol/L
  • ✅Clinical euvolaemia (no oedema, no dehydration)
  • ✅Normal adrenal, thyroid, renal function
02
Hypernatraemia — Almost Always Dehydration
Na >145 — usually loss of free water
▾
Causes
CategoryCausesUrine osmolality
Inadequate water intakeElderly, ICU patient, infants, impaired thirst mechanismHigh (>700)
Extra-renal water lossFever, sweating, burns, diarrhoea, tachypnoeaHigh (>700)
Renal water loss (Central DI)Hypothalamic/pituitary damage: trauma, tumour, surgery, SAHLow (<300) — responds to DDAVP
Renal water loss (Nephrogenic DI)Lithium, hypercalcaemia, hypokalemia, CKD, geneticLow–moderate — does NOT respond to DDAVP
Salt excessHypertonic saline, mineralocorticoid excess (Conn's, Cushing's)Urine Na high
⚠️ Correct hypernatraemia slowly — no faster than 10–12 mmol/L per 24h. Too rapid correction → cerebral oedema. Calculate free water deficit: 0.6 × weight (kg) × (Na/140 − 1). Replace over 48–72h using hypotonic fluids (0.45% saline or 5% dextrose).
03
Hypokalaemia — ECG Changes & Management
K <3.5 mmol/L — life-threatening at K <2.5
▾
Causes of hypokalaemia

GI losses

Vomiting (HCl loss → metabolic alkalosis → renal K wasting), diarrhoea, laxative abuse, ileostomy, fistulae, villous adenoma

Renal losses

Diuretics (thiazide, loop), mineralocorticoid excess (Conn's/Cushing's), Bartter/Gitelman syndrome, hypomagnesaemia, RTA type 1 & 2, polyuria phase of AKI

Intracellular shift

Alkalosis (0.6 mmol/L per 0.1 pH unit), insulin, β2-agonists (salbutamol), refeeding syndrome, hypokalaemic periodic paralysis, B12 therapy

ECG changes in hypokalaemia (in order of severity)
  • 📉T-wave flattening / inversion
  • 📉Prominent U waves (best seen V2–V4)
  • 📉ST depression
  • 🔴Prolonged QU interval (looks like long QT)
  • 🔴Ventricular ectopics → VT / VF (especially if on digoxin)
Replacement
Mild (3.0–3.5)
Oral KCl 40–80 mmol/day. Increase dietary potassium.
Moderate (2.5–3.0)
Oral if no symptoms. IV if symptomatic or not absorbing. Max IV rate 20 mmol/h peripherally.
Severe (<2.5) or arrhythmia
IV KCl via central line up to 40 mmol/h with continuous ECG monitoring. ICU setting.
⚠️ Always check and correct magnesium — hypomagnesaemia causes refractory hypokalaemia (Mg required for NKCC2 and ROMK function in kidney). K will not replace if Mg is low.
04
Hyperkalaemia — Cardiac Emergency Protocol
K >6.5 mmol/L = MEDICAL EMERGENCY — ECG immediately
▾
Causes of hyperkalaemia
  • 🔴AKI/CKD — reduced renal excretion (most common)
  • 🔴ACE inhibitors, ARBs, potassium-sparing diuretics, NSAIDs
  • 🟡Acidosis (0.6 mmol/L rise per 0.1 pH unit drop) — shifts K extracellularly
  • 🟡Addison's disease / hypoaldosteronism (type 4 RTA)
  • 🟡Massive cell lysis — rhabdomyolysis, tumour lysis syndrome, haemolysis, burns
  • 🟢Pseudohyperkalaemia — haemolysed sample, prolonged tourniquet, high platelets/WBC. Repeat sample!
ECG changes in hyperkalaemia (in order of severity)
  • 📈Peaked (tall tented) T waves — first sign (K ~5.5–6.5)
  • 📈Prolonged PR interval
  • 📈Widened QRS
  • 🔴P wave flattening / disappearance (sino-ventricular conduction)
  • 🔴Sine wave pattern → VF / asystole
Emergency management — ABCDE approach
StepDrug/InterventionEffect & Timing
A — Stabilise membrane10 mL 10% Calcium gluconate IV over 5–10 minCardioprotective within 1–3 min. Duration 30–60 min. Repeat if ECG unchanged after 5 min.
B — Shift K into cellsInsulin (10 units soluble) + 50 mL 50% glucose IV (or actrapid + dextrose)Lowers K by 0.5–1.5 mmol/L within 15–30 min. Duration 4–6h.
B — Shift K into cellsNebulised salbutamol 10–20 mgLowers K by 0.5–1.0 mmol/L. Synergistic with insulin. Avoid in cardiac arrhythmia.
B — Shift K into cells8.4% Sodium bicarbonate 50 mmol IV (if metabolic acidosis)Effective only if acidotic. Lowers K by ~0.5 mmol/L
C — Remove K from bodyFurosemide 40–80 mg IV (if not anuric)Renal excretion. Hours to days. Maintain urine output.
C — Remove K from bodySodium zirconium cyclosilicate (Lokelma) 10 g TDS × 48hBinds K in GI tract. Onset 1h. Or patiromer. Better tolerated than calcium resonium.
D — DialysisUrgent haemo/peritoneal dialysisFor refractory severe hyperkalaemia or anuric patient. Most effective removal method.
05
Calcium Disorders — Corrected Calcium Formula
Always correct for albumin before interpreting calcium
▾
💡 Corrected calcium formula: Corrected Ca²⁺ = Measured Ca²⁺ + 0.02 × (40 − albumin g/L). Low albumin → spuriously low calcium. Always use corrected value.
Hypercalcaemia (corrected Ca >2.62 mmol/L)
Causes — "Bones, Groans, Moans, Psychic Overtones"
Primary hyperparathyroidism (most common outpatient). Malignancy (most common inpatient): bone mets, PTHrP (solid tumours), myeloma, lymphoma (1,25-OH vitamin D). Vitamin D toxicity, sarcoidosis, granulomatous disease, milk-alkali, thiazides, thyrotoxicosis.
Symptoms by severity
LevelSymptoms
<3.0 mmol/LOften asymptomatic. Nausea, constipation, polyuria (nephrogenic DI)
3.0–3.5 mmol/LNausea, vomiting, confusion, lethargy, bone pain, abdominal pain
>3.5 mmol/L (crisis)Coma, renal failure, cardiac arrhythmia, pancreatitis — EMERGENCY
Management of hypercalcaemia crisis (>3.5 mmol/L)
  • 💧IV 0.9% saline: 3–4 L over 24h — volume expansion promotes calciuresis
  • 💊IV bisphosphonate: Zoledronic acid 4 mg or pamidronate 60–90 mg — onset 2–4 days, duration weeks
  • 💊Calcitonin 4–8 IU/kg IM/SC: Rapid onset (4–6h), tachyphylaxis after 48h
  • 💊Steroids: For sarcoidosis, vitamin D toxicity, haematological malignancy
  • 🏥Haemodialysis: For refractory or severe cases with renal failure
Hypocalcaemia (corrected Ca <2.12 mmol/L)
Causes
Hypoparathyroidism (post-thyroid/parathyroid surgery most common), vitamin D deficiency/resistance, hypomagnesaemia (refractory), pancreatitis (saponification), CKD, pseudohypoparathyroidism, hungry bone syndrome post-parathyroidectomy
Symptoms (Neuromuscular excitability)
Paraesthesiae (perioral, fingertips), tetany, Trousseau's sign (carpal spasm with BP cuff), Chvostek's sign (facial twitch), seizures, QT prolongation, stridor (laryngospasm)
Treatment
  • 🔴Symptomatic/severe: 10 mL 10% calcium gluconate IV over 10 min (contains 2.25 mmol Ca²⁺). Can repeat. Then infusion.
  • 🟡Mild/asymptomatic: Oral calcium carbonate 1–2 g/day + vitamin D (calcitriol if PTH deficient)
  • ✅Always check and replace magnesium — hypomagnesaemia causes refractory hypocalcaemia
06
Magnesium & Phosphate Disorders
Often overlooked but clinically critical — especially in ICU
▾
Hypomagnesaemia (Mg <0.7 mmol/L)
Causes
Poor intake (alcoholism, malnutrition), GI losses (diarrhoea, PPI use — chronic), renal losses (diuretics, cisplatin, amphotericin, aminoglycosides, EGFR inhibitors), refeeding syndrome
Clinical effects
Causes refractory hypokalaemia and hypocalcaemia. ECG: prolonged QT, TdP. Neuromuscular: tremor, seizures, ataxia. Hypomagnesaemia is the great electrolyte masquerader.
Hypophosphataemia (PO₄ <0.8 mmol/L)
Causes
Refeeding syndrome (classic — K, Mg, PO₄ all drop when nutrition restarted in malnourished), hyperparathyroidism (PTH promotes renal PO₄ wasting), antacid use (binds PO₄ in gut), DKA recovery, vitamin D deficiency, respiratory alkalosis
Severe effects (<0.3 mmol/L)
Haemolysis, rhabdomyolysis, encephalopathy, respiratory muscle weakness (failure to wean ventilator), cardiomyopathy, platelet dysfunction
⚠️ Refeeding syndrome: Malnourished patient given nutrition → insulin surge → cellular uptake of K, Mg, PO₄ → all three crash. Monitor closely for 72h after restarting nutrition. Restrict calories initially (10 kcal/kg/day) and supplement electrolytes proactively.
07
⚡ Emergency Quick Reference
Electrolytes critical findings at a glance
▾

⚡ Electrolytes — Emergency Quick Reference

Severe HyperkalaemiaECG changes (peaked T/wide QRS). Calcium gluconate → insulin/dextrose → salbutamol → dialysis
Hypercalcaemia Crisis (>3.5)IV saline + IV bisphosphonate + calcitonin. Check PTH + PTHrP + malignancy screen
Severe Hyponatraemia + Seizures3% hypertonic saline 150 mL bolus. Target 4–6 mmol/L rise in 6h. ICU. No faster than 10 mmol/L/24h total
Symptomatic Hypocalcaemia10 mL 10% calcium gluconate IV over 10 min. Then infusion. Check Mg.
Hyponatraemia + SIADHFluid restriction 500–700 mL/day. Tolvaptan if severe/refractory. Treat underlying cause
Refeeding SyndromeK/Mg/PO₄ all fall when feeding restarted. Monitor daily. Replace proactively. Restrict calories initially
DI (Diabetes Insipidus)Polyuria + hypernatraemia. Central DI: responds to DDAVP. Nephrogenic: treat cause (lithium, hypercalcaemia)
HypomagnesaemiaRefractory hypokalaemia/hypocalcaemia = check Mg. Replace first. IV MgSO₄ if symptomatic
🤖
Automated Interpretation
Upload any medical report — OCR extraction, smart parsing, rule-based + AI interpretation
📤 Upload
🔬 Parameters
📊 Interpretation
🩺 Diagnoses
💾 Export
✏️ Manual Report Entry
Select report type, then enter only the abnormal or relevant values. Leave blanks for normal ones.
Step 1 — Select Report Type
Step 2 — Enter Values (leave blank if normal)
📋
Drop your report here
Supports JPG, PNG, PDF — Lab reports, ECG tracings, CT scans, MRI images, Histopathology
Enter values directly without uploading an image

Uploaded Report

OCR Extraction

🧪
Detected Report Type
CBC — Complete Blood Count
98% match
OCR Confidence-
Extraction Confidence-
Extracted & Editable Parameters

Interpretation
Findings
Likely Diagnoses
⚠️ Educational aid only — not a medical diagnosis. Always consult a qualified clinician.
Export Report
Export the full interpreted report with parameters, findings, and diagnoses.
💡 PDF Export: Opens a professional medical report in a new tab — use Ctrl+P → Save as PDF. Poppins + Georgia typography, colour-coded results, A4 print-optimised.
❤️
CVS Examination
Cardiovascular System — systematic OSCE approach
01
General Inspection & Approach
From the end of the bed — before you touch the patient
▾
Before approaching
  • 🙋Introduce yourself, confirm patient identity, gain consent
  • 🛏️Position patient at 45° incline — standard for JVP assessment
  • 👁️Stand at foot of bed — scan for: breathlessness, pallor, cyanosis, distress, cachexia
General inspection — look for
🏥 Bedside clues
GTN spray, inhalers, oxygen, cardiac monitors, pacemaker box, GTN patch on chest
📋 Patient appearance
Malar flush (MS), cyanosis (central/peripheral), pallor, jaundice, cachexia (heart failure), diaphoresis
🫁 Breathing
Use of accessory muscles, pursed-lip breathing, Cheyne-Stokes respiration (severe HF)
💉 Lines/Devices
IVs, O2 delivery, cardiac telemetry leads, surgical scars visible from bed
02
Hands & Peripheral Signs
Nails → wrists → arms — a wealth of information
▾
Nails
Clubbing
Loss of nail-fold angle (Schamroth's sign). Causes: IE, cyanotic CHD, atrial myxoma
Splinter Haemorrhages
Linear dark streaks under nails. Infective endocarditis (also trauma)
Koilonychia
Spoon-shaped nails → iron deficiency anaemia
Leukonychia
White nails → hypoalbuminaemia (chronic HF, cirrhosis)
Palms & Hands
  • 🌡️Temperature: Warm/cool peripheries — check capillary refill (<2 sec normal)
  • 🔴Palmar erythema: High-output states (thyrotoxicosis, liver disease, pregnancy, HF)
  • 🔴Osler's nodes: Painful red nodules on finger pulps → Infective Endocarditis (immune complex)
  • 🔴Janeway lesions: Painless flat red macules on palms/soles → IE (septic emboli)
  • 💛Tendon xanthomata: Yellow deposits over tendons → familial hypercholesterolaemia
  • 🫀Dupuytren's contracture: Palmar fibrosis — associated with alcohol (dilated cardiomyopathy)
Radial Pulse
Rate
Count for 30 sec × 2. Normal 60–100 bpm. Report bradycardia (<60) or tachycardia (>100)
Rhythm
Regular / regularly irregular / irregularly irregular (AF). Count for 1 full minute if irregular
Character
Volume: full (aortic regurgitation) / low (shock, AS). Collapsing (waterhammer) = AR
Radio-radial delay
Check both radials simultaneously → delay = aortic arch pathology / coarctation
⚠️ Collapsing/waterhammer pulse test: Elevate patient's arm above heart level while feeling pulse — exaggerated impulse = aortic regurgitation. Always ask about pain first!
03
Blood Pressure & Brachial Pulse
Correct technique is essential for accuracy
▾
  • 💪Expose arm fully, apply cuff 2–3 cm above antecubital fossa, at heart level
  • 🔍Palpate brachial pulse first — estimate systolic before auscultation
  • 🩺Place stethoscope over brachial artery — listen for Korotkoff sounds
  • 📊Record both arms — >20 mmHg difference = aortic dissection / subclavian stenosis
  • 🏃Check for postural hypotension if clinically indicated (lying → standing → fall >20 systolic)
Pulsus paradoxus
SBP drops >10 mmHg on inspiration → cardiac tamponade, constrictive pericarditis, severe asthma
Pulsus alternans
Alternating strong/weak pulses → severe LV dysfunction
Wide pulse pressure
>60 mmHg → AR, thyrotoxicosis, anaemia, Paget's, AV fistula
Narrow pulse pressure
<25 mmHg → AS, tamponade, cardiogenic shock, hypovolaemia
04
Face & Neck Inspection
Eyes, mouth, JVP — don't rush this
▾
Eyes
  • 🟡Xanthelasma: Yellow plaques around eyelids → hypercholesterolaemia
  • ⬜Corneal arcus: White ring around cornea. In <50 yrs → familial hyperlipidaemia
  • 🔴Conjunctival pallor: Pull down lower lid → pale conjunctiva = anaemia → high-output HF, IE
  • 🟡Scleral icterus: Jaundice → haemolysis (prosthetic valve), right HF with hepatic congestion
  • 🔵Argyll Robertson pupils: Syphilitic aortitis (rare but examined!)
Mouth
  • 💙Central cyanosis: Blue lips/tongue = SaO2 <85% — significant hypoxia
  • ⬜Dental hygiene: Poor dentition → risk factor for infective endocarditis
  • 🔴High-arched palate: Marfan syndrome (AR, MVP, aortic dissection)
JVP — Jugular Venous Pressure
💡 Position at 45°. Turn head slightly away. Look for double waveform pulsation in the right internal jugular vein (not carotid — JVP is not palpable, obliterates with pressure, and changes with respiration)
Normal JVP
<3 cm above sternal angle. Double waveform (a + v waves). Decreases with inspiration
Raised JVP
Right HF, cor pulmonale, tamponade, SVCO, fluid overload, constrictive pericarditis
Absent a-wave
Atrial fibrillation (no coordinated atrial contraction)
Giant v-wave
Tricuspid regurgitation — systolic pulsation in neck veins
Cannon a-wave
Complete heart block, VT, junctional rhythm (atrium contracts against closed TV)
Kussmaul's sign
JVP rises on inspiration → constrictive pericarditis, restrictive cardiomyopathy
⚠️ Hepatojugular reflex: Apply firm pressure over liver for 15 sec — JVP rises >3 cm = right heart failure / tricuspid regurgitation
05
Precordial Inspection & Palpation
Before the stethoscope goes on
▾
Inspect the precordium
  • 🔍Chest deformity — pectus excavatum (MVP, Marfan), pectus carinatum
  • ⭐Visible apex beat or abnormal pulsations
  • 🪡Surgical scars — sternotomy (CABG, valve replacement), lateral thoracotomy (mitral valvotomy), pacemaker scar (left infraclavicular)
  • 📍Pacemaker/ICD device bulge below left clavicle
  • 💧Visible chest wall pulsations — right ventricular heave
Apex Beat — Palpation
Location (Normal)
5th intercostal space, midclavicular line. Use flat of hand first, then fingertips
Displaced laterally
LV dilation → volume overload (AR, MR), dilated cardiomyopathy
Heaving / Sustained
LV hypertrophy → AS, hypertension (pressure overload)
Hyperdynamic
Vigorous but not sustained → volume overload (AR, MR, high-output)
Double impulse
HOCM — pre-systolic impulse from atrial kick + systolic impulse
Impalpable
Obesity, COPD (barrel chest), pericardial effusion, dextrocardia
Other Palpation Points
  • ✋Parasternal heave (left sternal edge): Right ventricular hypertrophy (cor pulmonale, MS, PE, pulmonary HTN)
  • ✋Palpable P2 (pulmonary area): Pulmonary hypertension
  • 📳Thrills: Palpable murmurs — grade ≥4/6 by definition. Systolic thrill at base = AS or PS. Diastolic thrill at apex = MS
06
Auscultation — Heart Sounds & Murmurs
The core skill — systematic 4-area approach
▾
Auscultation Areas (in order)
① Aortic Area
2nd ICS, right sternal border. Use diaphragm. Best for AS
② Pulmonary Area
2nd ICS, left sternal border. Best for PS, pulmonary regurgitation
③ Tricuspid Area
4th–5th ICS, left sternal border. Best for TR, TS
④ Mitral Area (Apex)
5th ICS, MCL. Bell for low-freq sounds (MS, S3, S4), diaphragm for others
Normal Heart Sounds
  • 🔴S1 (Lub): Mitral + tricuspid valve CLOSURE. Best at apex. Loud in MS (early), soft in MR, long PR
  • 🔵S2 (Dub): Aortic + pulmonary valve CLOSURE. Best at base. Listen for splitting
  • 🔵A2-P2 splitting: Physiological on inspiration. Fixed splitting = ASD. Paradoxical = LBBB, AS
Added Heart Sounds
S3 (Ventricular gallop)
Early diastole, low-pitched, apex with bell. Volume overload: HF, MR, AR, hyperkinetic states. Normal in young/pregnancy
S4 (Atrial gallop)
Late diastole (pre-systolic), low-pitched. Stiff, non-compliant LV. HTN, AS, HOCM, HCM. Never normal
Opening Snap (OS)
High-pitched, early diastole, medial to apex. Mitral stenosis. Closer to S2 = more severe MS
Ejection Click
Early systole after S1. Aortic/pulmonary — bicuspid AV, PS. Moves with respiration if pulmonary
Mid-systolic Click
Apex, followed by late systolic murmur = MVP (mitral valve prolapse)
Pericardial friction rub
Scratchy, 3-component, best leaning forward at expiration. Pericarditis. Varies with position
Murmur Assessment — Always Report ALL of These
FeatureWhat to say
TimingSystolic / Diastolic / Continuous
Grade1 (barely audible) → 6 (audible without stethoscope)
LocationWhere loudest — apex / LSE / base / axilla
RadiationAxilla (MR), neck/carotids (AS), back (PS/coarctation)
CharacterHarsh / blowing / rumbling / high-pitched / low-pitched
ManoeuvresValsalva, handgrip, squat→stand, leaning forward, left lateral
Classic Murmur Summary
MurmurTimingBest HeardRadiationCharacter
Aortic StenosisEjection systolicAortic areaCarotidsHarsh, crescendo-decrescendo
Aortic RegurgitationEarly diastolicLSE, leaning forward—Blowing, decrescendo
Mitral StenosisMid-diastolicApex, left lateral—Rumbling, low-pitched
Mitral RegurgitationPan-systolicApexAxillaBlowing, uniform
Tricuspid RegurgitationPan-systolicLower LSE—Increases with inspiration (Carvallo's)
HOCMEjection systolicLSE + apex—Increases with Valsalva / decreases with squat
PDAContinuousLeft infraclavicular—Machinery murmur
VSDPan-systolicLSE 3rd–4th ICS—Harsh, loud
💡 Auscultate axilla for MR radiation, then carotids for AS radiation. Always listen with patient leaning forward (expiration held) for AR. Left lateral decubitus position for MS.
07
Lung Bases & Peripheral Oedema
Complete the CVS exam
▾
Lung bases (posterior)
  • 🫁Percuss from shoulder → base bilaterally — dullness = pleural effusion (HF)
  • 🩺Auscultate both bases — fine bibasal crepitations = pulmonary oedema (HF)
  • 🔊Coarse crackles + bronchial breathing = consolidation/pneumonia (rule out)
Peripheral Oedema
  • 👣Inspect ankles/shins — bilateral pitting oedema = right HF / hypoalbuminaemia / venous insufficiency
  • 👆Press firmly over tibia/sacrum (bedridden patients) for 5 seconds — pitting = fluid overload
  • 📏Document extent — ankle / mid-shin / knee / thigh
  • 📍Unilateral oedema → DVT (not cardiac) — check Homan's sign (dorsiflexion calf pain)
Abdomen (hepatomegaly / splenomegaly)
  • 🫀Hepatomegaly: Tender, pulsatile (TR), smooth enlarged liver = right HF / hepatic congestion
  • 🔴Splenomegaly: Infective endocarditis (septic emboli), portal hypertension
  • 🩸Ascites: Right HF, constrictive pericarditis — shifting dullness, fluid thrill
🚨 Always complete your exam by saying: "To complete my CVS examination I would also check: oxygen saturations, 12-lead ECG, CXR, and request relevant bloods (BNP, troponin, U&E, FBC, LFTs)."
🫁
Respiratory Examination
Systematic OSCE approach — inspection to auscultation
01
General Inspection
From the end of the bed — appearance, environment, breathing pattern
▾
  • 🙋Introduce, confirm ID, gain consent. Position at 45°
  • 🏥Bedside: Inhalers, spacers, nebuliser, O2 mask, sputum pot, peak flow meter, smoking history
  • 😰Distress: Dyspnoea at rest, inability to complete sentences, pursed-lip breathing
  • 💙Cyanosis: Central (tongue/lips → SaO2 <85%) vs peripheral (fingertips → vasoconstriction)
  • 📏Chest shape: Barrel chest (COPD — AP = transverse diameter), pectus excavatum/carinatum, kyphoscoliosis (restrictive defect), Harrison's sulcus (childhood asthma)
  • 🔄Chest movement: Symmetrical? Unequal expansion = pneumothorax, pleural effusion, pneumonia, collapse on that side
  • ⚡Use of accessory muscles: SCM, scalenes, intercostals — indicates respiratory distress
  • 🔁Breathing pattern: Cheyne-Stokes (HF/CNS), Kussmaul (DKA), paradoxical (flail chest)
  • 🔊Audible sounds: Stridor (upper airway obstruction), wheeze (lower obstruction)
02
Hands, Arms & Peripheral Signs
Nails → wrists → radial pulse → tremor
▾
Clubbing
Causes: Fibrosis (IPF), bronchiectasis, empyema, lung cancer, mesothelioma, cystic fibrosis
Peripheral cyanosis
Bluish fingertips → vasoconstriction or desaturation
Nicotine staining
Yellow-brown nails — smoking (COPD, lung cancer risk)
Wrist flap (asterixis)
CO2 retention — ask patient to extend wrists >15 sec. Flapping = type 2 resp failure (COPD)
  • 💊Fine tremor: Beta-2 agonist overuse (salbutamol) — always check medication history
  • 🫀Radial pulse: Rate, rhythm. Tachycardia = infection/PE/distress. Bounding = CO2 retention
  • 🔴Lymph nodes: Axillary — malignancy, TB
03
Face & Neck
Eyes, mouth, trachea, lymph nodes
▾
  • 💙Central cyanosis: Tongue and lips — clinically evident at SaO2 <85%
  • 👁️Conjunctival pallor: Anaemia — worsens dyspnoea
  • 👁️Horner's syndrome: Ptosis + miosis + anhidrosis → Pancoast tumour (lung apex)
  • 😮Pursed-lip breathing: COPD — keeps airways open by creating back-pressure
  • 🗣️Hoarse voice: Recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy — lung cancer, aortic aneurysm
Trachea — Critical Step
⚠️ Feel for tracheal position: Place index finger in suprasternal notch. Central = normal. Deviated TOWARDS lesion = collapse/fibrosis. Deviated AWAY = tension pneumothorax, large effusion, large mass
  • 📏Cricosternal distance: <3 cm = hyperinflation (COPD, asthma)
  • 🔴Cervical lymphadenopathy: TB, sarcoidosis, lymphoma, lung cancer metastases — palpate all chains
  • 🌊Tracheal tug: Trachea pulls down on inspiration = severe airflow obstruction / hyperinflation
  • 🫀Raised JVP: Cor pulmonale (chronic hypoxic lung disease), SVC obstruction (lung cancer)
04
Chest Expansion
Compare anterior then posterior — both sides
▾
  • 🤲Place thumbs at xiphisternum (anterior) or spinous process (posterior), fingers wrap around sides
  • 📏Thumbs should separate symmetrically ≥5 cm on deep inspiration
  • ⚖️Reduced bilaterally: COPD, asthma, neuromuscular (poor inspiratory effort)
  • ⚖️Reduced unilaterally: Pleural effusion, pneumothorax, consolidation/collapse on THAT side
  • 🔼Upper zone expansion (hands at clavicles) — reduced = apical fibrosis (TB, sarcoid), Pancoast
05
Percussion
Map the lungs — compare side to side
▾
Technique
  • ✋Place middle finger of non-dominant hand flat on chest wall, parallel to ribs
  • 🔨Strike middle phalanx briskly with tip of dominant middle finger — use wrist flick
  • 🔄Percuss apex → base comparing LEFT vs RIGHT at each level. Include lateral chest walls
  • 📍Percuss anteriorly (2nd, 4th, 6th ICS), posteriorly (T2, T4, T6, T8 levels), axillae
Percussion Notes — Differential Diagnosis
NoteQualityCauses
ResonantNormal hollow soundNormal lung
HyperresonantBooming / drum-likePneumothorax, emphysema (hyperinflation)
DullThud — like percussing thighConsolidation (pneumonia), collapse, tumour
Stony dullAbsolute dullness — no resonancePleural effusion (fluid replaces air)
Cardiac dullnessDull left anterior chestNormal — cardiac shadow. Increased = pericardial effusion
💡 Percuss the liver dullness (right side, below 5th ICS anteriorly) — hyperresonance over liver = hyperinflation. Liver descended below rib margin in COPD
06
Auscultation — Breath Sounds & Added Sounds
The most information-dense step
▾
Technique
  • 🩺Use diaphragm — compare left vs right at same levels (apex → base, anterior, posterior, axillae)
  • 😮‍💨Ask patient to breathe through mouth — normal quiet breathing, then deep breaths if needed
  • 🔄Listen for full cycle (inspiration + expiration) at each location
Breath Sound Types
SoundQualityNormal LocationAbnormal = ?
VesicularSoft, rustling. Insp > expMost lung fields—
BronchialHarsh, tubular. Exp ≥ insp. Pause betweenOnly over trachea/manubriumConsolidation, collapse + patent bronchus, fibrosis
BronchovesicularIntermediateHilar regionsConsolidation at hilar level
Absent / ReducedQuiet or inaudibleNever normal peripherallyEffusion, pneumothorax, collapse, obesity, poor effort
Added (Adventitious) Sounds
Fine Crackles (crepitations)
Late inspiratory, basal. Pulmonary fibrosis (Velcro-like), pulmonary oedema. Don't clear with cough
Coarse Crackles
Early inspiratory, any level. Bronchiectasis, pneumonia, COPD. May change with cough
Wheeze (rhonchi)
High-pitched, musical. Expiratory predominant. Asthma, COPD. Polyphonic = widespread obstruction
Monophonic wheeze
Single pitch — fixed partial obstruction: foreign body, endobronchial tumour
Stridor
Inspiratory high-pitched — upper airway obstruction: croup, epiglottitis, foreign body, tumour. EMERGENCY if new
Pleural Rub
Creaking, leathery, both phases. Pleuritis — PE, pneumonia, TB, malignancy
Vocal Resonance (Tactile Fremitus)
  • 🗣️Ask patient to say "99" repeatedly. Place stethoscope and compare sides
  • 📢Increased resonance: Consolidation (sound conducts well through solid lung) — bronchophony, aegophony ("e" → "A")
  • 🔇Decreased resonance: Pleural effusion (fluid absorbs sound), pneumothorax, collapse
  • 🔊Whispering pectoriloquy: Whispered "99" clearly audible → consolidation
🚨 To complete your exam: SpO2 monitoring, peak flow, sputum examination, CXR, CT thorax if indicated, ABGs (if type 2 RF suspected), spirometry
07
Classic Respiratory Exam Syndromes
Quick-reference pattern recognition
▾
ConditionExpansionTracheaPercussionBreath SoundsVR
NormalEqualCentralResonantVesicularNormal
Consolidation↓ ipsilateralCentralDullBronchial↑
Pleural Effusion↓ ipsilateralAway (if large)Stony dullAbsent (± rub)↓
Pneumothorax↓ ipsilateralAway (if tension)HyperresonantAbsent↓
Collapse↓ ipsilateralTowardsDullAbsent/bronchial↓
Fibrosis↓ bilaterallyTowards (if unilateral)DullBronchial + fine crackles↑
COPD/Asthma↓ bilaterallyCentral (↓ cricosternal)Hyperresonant↓ Vesicular + wheeze↓
🧠
CNS Examination
Neurological system — cranial nerves, motor, sensory, coordination & reflexes
01
General Inspection & Mental State
Before detailed neuro exam — always start here
▾
  • 🙋Introduce, gain consent. Note: orientation in time, place and person as you speak
  • 👁️Appearance: Facial asymmetry, facial palsy, ptosis, abnormal eye movements, abnormal posture
  • 🗣️Speech: Dysarthria (slurred = cerebellar/UMN), dysphasia (expressive/receptive/nominal — dominant hemisphere)
  • 🔄Involuntary movements: Tremor (resting vs intention), chorea, hemiballismus, myoclonus, tics, fasciculations
  • 🚶Gait (if ambulatory): Hemiplegic (circumduction), cerebellar (wide-based, ataxic), Parkinsonian (stooped, shuffling, festinant), high-stepping (foot drop), waddling (proximal myopathy), antalgic
GCS (Glasgow Coma Scale)
Eye (1–4) + Verbal (1–5) + Motor (1–6) = /15. Always document separately, not just total
MMSE / ACE-III
Orientation, registration, attention, recall, language, visuospatial. <24/30 = cognitive impairment
02
Cranial Nerves I–VI
Olfactory → Abducens — systematic testing
▾
CNNameTestAbnormal finding → Lesion
IOlfactoryEach nostril — smell coffee/peppermint (not ammonia!)Anosmia → frontal lobe tumour, head injury, COVID-19, meningioma
IIOpticVisual acuity (Snellen), visual fields (confrontation), colour vision, fundoscopy, RAPDRAPD = optic neuritis (MS). Field defects → localise the lesion
IIIOculomotorPupil response to light and accommodation, eye movements (H-pattern), ptosisFixed dilated pupil + ptosis = CN3 palsy (aneurysm PCOA, herniation)
IVTrochlearDownward/inward eye movement (looking down stairs), vertical diplopiaHead tilt to compensate → superior oblique palsy
VTrigeminalSensation V1/V2/V3 (forehead/cheek/chin) with cotton wool + pinprick. Motor: jaw clench + open against resistance. Corneal reflexFacial numbness → brainstem, cerebellopontine angle, cavernous sinus
VIAbducensLateral eye movement — failure to abduct → convergent squintFalse localising sign of raised ICP. Also MS, Wernicke's, pontine lesion
Visual Field Defects — Key Patterns
Monocular blindness
Retina or optic nerve (before chiasm). Ipsilateral eye affected
Bitemporal hemianopia
Optic chiasm compression — pituitary adenoma, craniopharyngioma
Homonymous hemianopia
Optic tract/radiation/occipital cortex — MCA stroke, PCA stroke
Superior quadrantanopia
Temporal lobe (Meyer's loop) — "pie in the sky"
Inferior quadrantanopia
Parietal lobe radiation — "pie on the floor"
Macular sparing
Occipital cortex (dual blood supply) — posterior cerebral artery infarct
03
Cranial Nerves VII–XII
Facial → Hypoglossal — lower cranial nerves
▾
CNNameTestAbnormal → Lesion
VIIFacialRaise eyebrows, screw eyes shut (against resistance), puff cheeks, show teeth, smile. Taste (anterior ⅔ tongue)UMN: forehead spared (bilateral cortical supply). LMN: forehead involved → Bell's palsy, parotid tumour, acoustic neuroma
VIIIVestibulocochlearHearing — whisper test (2 feet, repeat numbers). Rinne's test (fork 512 Hz on mastoid vs air). Weber's test (fork on vertex)Rinne's: BC>AC = conductive deafness. Weber lateralises to bad ear = conductive; good ear = sensorineural. Acoustic neuroma
IX & XGlossopharyngeal & VagusSay "Ahh" — palate rises symmetrically. Gag reflex (afferent IX, efferent X). Swallowing. Hoarse voice (X)Palate deviates AWAY from lesion. Absent gag = dysphagia risk. Unilateral vocal cord = recurrent laryngeal nerve palsy
XIAccessoryShrug shoulders against resistance (trapezius). Turn head against resistance (sternocleidomastoid)Weakness on ipsilateral side. Jugular foramen syndrome (IX, X, XI together)
XIIHypoglossalProtrude tongue — straight? Any wasting? Fasciculations?UMN: tongue deviates AWAY from lesion. LMN: deviates TOWARDS lesion (ipsilateral), with wasting + fasciculations
💡 Rinne's Mnemonic: Rinne's Positive (AC>BC) = Normal OR Sensorineural deafness. Rinne's Negative (BC>AC) = Conductive deafness. Weber lateralises towards worse ear in conductive, towards better ear in sensorineural.
04
Motor Examination — Upper Limbs
Inspection → tone → power → reflexes
▾
Inspection
  • 👁️Wasting (LMN / disuse), fasciculations (LMN — ALS), tremor, abnormal posture
  • ✋Pronator drift: arms outstretched, eyes closed, 30 sec → ipsilateral pronation + drift = UMN pyramidal weakness
Tone
  • 🔄Passively flex/extend elbow and wrist — examine relaxed limb
  • ⬆️Increased tone: Spasticity (clasp-knife) = UMN lesion. Rigidity (lead-pipe, cogwheel) = Parkinson's, extrapyramidal
  • ⬇️Decreased tone: Hypotonia = LMN lesion, cerebellar lesion, acute spinal shock
Power — MRC Scale
GradeDescription
0No contraction
1Flicker / visible contraction, no movement
2Movement with gravity eliminated
3Movement against gravity but not resistance
4Movement against some resistance (4−/4/4+)
5Normal power
Upper Limb Power Testing
Shoulder abduction (C5)
Deltoid — "push arms out to sides, don't let me push them down"
Elbow flexion (C5,C6)
Biceps — "pull arm up, don't let me straighten it"
Elbow extension (C7)
Triceps — "push arm straight, don't let me bend it"
Wrist extension (C7)
Finger/wrist extensors
Grip (C8)
"Squeeze my fingers as hard as you can"
Finger spread (T1)
Interossei — "spread fingers, don't let me push them together"
Upper Limb Reflexes
Biceps (C5,C6)
Thumb on tendon at antecubital fossa — strike thumb
Supinator (C5,C6)
Forearm pronated, tap brachioradialis tendon
Triceps (C7)
Tap triceps tendon above olecranon, arm at 90°
Finger jerk (C8)
Tap flexed fingers — positive Hoffmann's = UMN
⚠️ Reflexes: Grade as 0 (absent), + (reduced), ++ (normal), +++ (brisk), ++++ (clonus). Absent + fasciculations = LMN. Brisk + clonus + Babinski = UMN.
05
Motor Examination — Lower Limbs
Tone → power → reflexes → Babinski
▾
Tone
  • 🔄Roll thigh from side to side — foot moves freely = normal. Rigid = increased tone
  • 🦵Rapidly flex/extend knee and hip — assess resistance
  • ⚡Ankle clonus: sharply dorsiflex ankle and sustain — >3 beats = UMN pathology
Lower Limb Power Testing
Hip flexion (L1,L2)
"Lift your leg off the bed, don't let me push it down" — iliopsoas
Hip extension (L4,L5)
Gluteus maximus — push leg back into bed against resistance
Knee flexion (L5,S1)
Hamstrings — "pull heel towards buttocks"
Knee extension (L3,L4)
Quadriceps — "straighten knee, don't let me bend it"
Ankle dorsiflexion (L4,L5)
Tibialis anterior — "pull foot up towards you"
Ankle plantarflexion (S1,S2)
"Push foot down like pressing accelerator"
Big toe extension (L5)
Extensor hallucis longus — key L5 root test
Hip abduction / adduction
L4/L5 abduction (gluteus medius). L2/L3 adduction (adductors)
Lower Limb Reflexes
Knee jerk (L3,L4)
Tap patellar tendon — quadriceps contraction
Ankle jerk (S1,S2)
Dorsiflex foot slightly, tap Achilles tendon
Plantar reflex (L5,S1)
Stroke lateral sole heel → toes. Flexion = normal. Babinski (extension + fanning) = UMN lesion
Cremasteric reflex (L1,L2)
Stroke inner thigh → testis elevates. Absent = cord lesion above L1
🚨 Upgoing plantar (Babinski sign) = UMN lesion above L5/S1. Always document which foot. Both upgoing = bilateral UMN — cord lesion, bilateral strokes, cerebral palsy
06
Sensory Examination
Light touch, pinprick, vibration, proprioception, temperature
▾
Sensory Modalities — What They Test
Light touch (cotton wool)
Dorsal columns + spinothalamic. Compare distal → proximal, dermatomal pattern
Pinprick (neurotip)
Lateral spinothalamic tract (crosses immediately). "Sharp or dull?" Test same levels
Temperature
Lateral spinothalamic. Tuning fork (cold metal) or hot/cold tubes
Vibration (128 Hz fork)
Dorsal columns (ascend ipsilaterally). Start at big toe → ankle → knee → ASIS if impaired
Proprioception (Joint position sense)
Dorsal columns. Hold lateral surfaces of distal phalanx. Move up/down with eyes closed. "Which way?" Start distally
Two-point discrimination
Cortical sensation — parietal lobe. Normal fingertip <5 mm
Sensory Level — Spinal Cord Localisation
LevelLandmark
C4Clavicles (cape area)
T4Nipple line
T6Xiphisternum
T10Umbilicus
T12/L1Inguinal ligament
L3Anterior knee
L4Medial shin / great toe
L5Lateral shin / dorsum foot / big toe
S1Lateral foot / sole
S3–S5Perianal / saddle area
💡 Dissociated sensory loss: Spinothalamic (pain/temp) lost but dorsal column (vibration/proprioception) intact at same level → syringomyelia, Brown-Séquard syndrome. The reverse pattern = posterior column lesion (subacute combined degeneration, B12 deficiency)
07
Coordination & Cerebellar Function
DANISH mnemonic — all cerebellar signs
▾
DANISH Mnemonic
D — Dysdiadochokinesia
Rapid alternating movements (pronation/supination) — irregular, slow = cerebellar
A — Ataxia (gait)
Wide-based, unsteady, unable to tandem walk. Ipsilateral cerebellar lesion
N — Nystagmus
Fast phase towards lesion (horizontal). Ask to follow finger to extremes of gaze
I — Intention tremor
Worsens as target approaches (finger-nose test). Absent at rest
S — Scanning dysarthria
Explosive, staccato speech ("British Constitution") — ask patient to repeat
H — Hypotonia
Pendular knee jerk, reduced limb tone — ipsilateral to lesion
Coordination Tests
  • 👆Finger-nose test: Touch nose → examiner's finger (move it!) → nose. Slow, watch for past-pointing and intention tremor
  • 🦵Heel-shin test: Place heel on opposite knee, slide down shin to ankle. Smooth? Veering = ataxia
  • 🤲Dysdiadochokinesia: Rapid pronation/supination on palm of other hand as fast as possible
  • 🧍Romberg's test: Stand with feet together, eyes open then closed. Falls with eyes closed = dorsal column / proprioceptive deficit (not cerebellar — cerebellar ataxia is same with both eyes)
  • 🚶Tandem walk: Heel-to-toe in straight line. Ataxia = cerebellar or vestibular
⚠️ Romberg positive (positive = FALLS with eyes closed): Dorsal column disease (B12 deficiency, tabes dorsalis, MS). NOT a cerebellar test — cerebellar patients fall with eyes open AND closed.
08
UMN vs LMN — Localising the Lesion
The fundamental distinction in all of neurology
▾
FeatureUMN (Upper Motor Neurone)LMN (Lower Motor Neurone)
WastingMinimal / disuse onlyPronounced, early
ToneIncreased (spasticity/rigidity)Decreased (flaccid)
PowerPyramidal pattern weaknessFollows nerve/root distribution
ReflexesBrisk, clonusReduced / absent
PlantarExtensor (Babinski +ve)Flexor (normal)
FasciculationsAbsentPresent (LMN hallmark)
UMN Localisation
Cortex (motor cortex)
Contralateral face + arm > leg weakness (MCA territory). Homunculus distribution
Internal capsule
Dense contralateral hemiplegia. Thalamus often involved (hemisensory loss)
Brainstem
Ipsilateral CN deficit + contralateral hemiplegia = crossed signs. Weber's / Wallenberg's syndromes
Spinal cord
Bilateral UMN below level. Brown-Séquard = ipsilateral UMN + dorsal column loss, contralateral spinothalamic loss
🚨 To complete your CNS examination: Fundoscopy (papilloedema in raised ICP), bedside cognitive testing (MMSE), gait assessment, sphincter assessment if cord lesion suspected, CT head / MRI brain/spine, and full bloods (B12, glucose, TFTs, syphilis serology if indicated)
🧑‍⚕️
General Physical Examination
Systematic head-to-toe assessment — the foundation of all clinical examinations
01
Introduction & First Impression
Even before you touch the patient — observe everything
▾
  • 🤝Introduce yourself, confirm patient identity (name, DOB), explain the examination, gain verbal consent
  • 👁️General appearance: Well/unwell, distressed/comfortable, obese/cachectic, jaundiced/pale/cyanosed/flushed
  • 🛏️Posture & environment: Sitting upright vs. orthopnoea, oxygen mask, drips, drains, monitoring equipment
  • 🗣️Speech: Normal, dysarthric, dysphonic, dysphasic
  • 🧠Mental state: Alert, drowsy, confused, agitated — document GCS if relevant
Nutritional Status
Wasting (temporal, thenar), cachexia (malignancy, TB, cardiac), obesity — note distribution (central vs peripheral)
Hydration
Dry mucous membranes, skin turgor (pinch), sunken eyes, postural hypotension → dehydration
02
Vital Signs
The six vital signs — measure, interpret, act
▾
60–100
HR (bpm)
90–140 / 60–90
BP (mmHg)
12–20
RR (breaths/min)
36.5–37.5°C
Temp
≥95%
SpO₂
4–11 mmol/L
BG (fasting)
Vital SignInterpretationClinical Action
HR >100Tachycardia — pain, fever, hypovolaemia, arrhythmia, anxiety, drugsECG, fluid status, sepsis screen
HR <60Bradycardia — drugs (β-blocker, digoxin), athletic, heart block, hypothyroidismECG urgently if symptomatic
BP <90/60Hypotension — sepsis, haemorrhage, cardiac failure, Addison's, neurogenic shockIV access, fluids, urgent review
BP ≥140/90Hypertension — essential, CKD, Cushing's, phaeochromocytoma, coarctationCheck other limb, fundoscopy, urine
RR >20Tachypnoea — infection, PE, acidosis, pain, anxiety, cardiac failureMost sensitive early sepsis marker
Temp >38°CFever — infection, malignancy, autoimmune, drugs, pulmonary embolismBlood cultures × 2, CRP, WBC, source
⚠️ ALWAYS measure BP in both arms — a difference >15 mmHg suggests aortic dissection or subclavian stenosis. RR is the most sensitive early warning sign of deterioration — document it properly.
03
Hands & Nails
A window into systemic disease — examine both hands
▾
Nails
Clubbing
Loss of nail fold angle (>180°). Causes: lung CA, fibrosing alveolitis, bronchiectasis, cyanotic CHD, IBD, cirrhosis, infective endocarditis
Koilonychia
Spoon-shaped nails → iron deficiency anaemia
Leukonychia
White nails → hypoalbuminaemia (liver disease, nephrotic syndrome)
Beau's lines
Transverse ridges = past systemic illness (note timing by distance from matrix)
Splinter haemorrhages
Infective endocarditis, vasculitis, trauma
Onycholysis
Nail separation from bed → psoriasis, thyrotoxicosis, fungal infection
Palms & Dorsum
Palmar erythema
Liver cirrhosis, pregnancy, thyrotoxicosis, RA, polycythaemia
Dupuytren's contracture
Palmar fascia fibrosis → ring/little finger. Liver disease, Peyronie's, idiopathic
Thenar wasting
Median nerve compression (carpal tunnel), T1 root lesion, motor neurone disease
Asterixis (flap)
Outstretched hands: coarse flapping tremor = hepatic encephalopathy, CO₂ retention, uraemia
04
Face, Eyes & Mouth
Critical signs in the face — never skip
▾
Eyes
Jaundice (sclerae)
Bilirubin >35 µmol/L. Pre-hepatic, hepatic, post-hepatic — follow the algorithm
Anaemia (conjunctiva)
Pallor of lower palpebral conjunctiva. Haemoglobin <~80 g/L to see clinically
Xanthelasma
Periorbital cholesterol deposits → hyperlipidaemia (but can be normolipidaemic)
Corneal arcus
White ring — significant if <50 yrs → hypercholesterolaemia
Kayser-Fleischer rings
Copper deposits at corneal margin → Wilson's disease (needs slit-lamp)
Exophthalmos
Thyrotoxicosis (Graves') — lid lag, lid retraction, chemosis
Mouth & Mucous Membranes
  • 🔵Central cyanosis: Bluish tongue/lips → SpO₂ <85%. Peripheral = cold hands only (vasospasm/vasoconstriction)
  • 🟡Angular stomatitis / glossitis: Iron, B12, folate, riboflavin deficiency
  • ⬜Oral candidiasis: Immunosuppression (HIV, steroids, chemotherapy)
  • 🟫Pigmented buccal mucosa: Addison's disease (also scars, gums)
05
Lymph Nodes & Neck
Systematic lymph node examination — head, neck, axillae, groins
▾
Cervical Lymph Node Groups (examine from behind)
Submental & Submandibular
Floor of mouth, lip, cheek pathology. Dental infection
Anterior cervical
Tonsillar → tonsillitis, pharyngitis, EBV
Posterior cervical
EBV/CMV, TB, lymphoma, scalp infection
Supraclavicular
Always pathological. Left (Virchow's = Troisier's) → gastric/abdominal malignancy. Right → lung/mediastinal
Axillary
Breast cancer, upper limb infection, lymphoma
Inguinal
Lower limb, perineal pathology. Reactive common. Fixed/hard → malignancy
⚠️ Hard, fixed, non-tender, rubbery lymph nodes = malignancy until proven otherwise. Soft, tender, fluctuant = infection/reactive. Always examine all groups — generalised lymphadenopathy = EBV, lymphoma, HIV, CMV, sarcoid
Thyroid
  • 👀Inspect from front — any midline swelling (goitre)? Asymmetry?
  • 💧Ask to swallow water — thyroid and thyroglossal cysts rise. Lymph nodes do NOT
  • 🖐️Palpate from behind — lobes, isthmus, consistency, tender, nodular?
  • 🔊Auscultate for bruit → Graves' hyperthyroidism (increased vascularity)
06
Oedema & Peripheral Signs
Pitting vs non-pitting — always examine legs and sacrum
▾

Pitting Oedema

Finger pressure leaves pit. Causes: cardiac failure (↑venous pressure), hypoalbuminaemia (nephrotic, cirrhosis, malnutrition), lymphoedema, venous insufficiency, drugs (CCBs, NSAIDs)

Non-Pitting Oedema

Does NOT pit. Causes: lymphoedema (post-radiotherapy, malignancy, filariasis), myxoedema (hypothyroidism — cool, doughy, non-pitting pretibial)

Grading Pitting Oedema
GradeDepthRecoveryExtent
1+2 mmImmediateFoot/ankle only
2+4 mm<15 secBelow knee
3+6 mm1–2 minTo knee
4+8 mm+>2 minGeneralised/anasarca
💡 Always examine the sacrum in bed-bound patients — gravity-dependent oedema collects there, not the ankles. Bilateral oedema = systemic. Unilateral = local (DVT, lymphoedema, cellulitis)
📋
History Taking
Structured clinical history — the most powerful diagnostic tool in medicine
01
Opening & Patient Details
Set the scene — establish rapport first
▾
  • 🤝Introduce yourself: name, role, purpose of consultation
  • 🪑Ensure comfort, privacy and appropriate environment. Offer chaperone if needed
  • 📝Confirm: Name, Age/DOB, Occupation, Handedness (neurology) — document before starting
  • 🗣️Open question: "What's brought you in today?" or "Tell me about your problem" — then LISTEN for 2 minutes uninterrupted
02
Presenting Complaint & History of Presenting Complaint
Use SOCRATES + ICE to explore every symptom
▾
SOCRATES Mnemonic (for pain/any symptom)
S — Site
Where exactly? Ask patient to point with one finger. Localised vs diffuse?
O — Onset
When did it start? Sudden (seconds) vs rapid (hours) vs gradual (days/weeks)? What were you doing?
C — Character
What does it feel like? Sharp, dull, aching, burning, crushing, throbbing, colicky?
R — Radiation
Does it go anywhere? Arm/jaw (MI), back (aortic dissection), groin (renal colic), shoulder tip (diaphragm)
A — Associations
Other symptoms? Nausea, vomiting, sweating, dyspnoea, fever, change in bowel/bladder
T — Time course
Constant vs intermittent? Getting better, worse or same? Any previous episodes?
E — Exacerbating/Relieving
What makes it worse/better? Movement, food, GTN, rest, posture, breathing, defaecation?
S — Severity
Pain score 0–10. Compared to worst ever? Preventing daily activities? Waking from sleep?
ICE — Ideas, Concerns, Expectations

Ideas

"What do you think is causing your symptoms?" — patient's own understanding and health beliefs

Concerns

"Is there anything you are particularly worried about?" — what fears does the patient have?

Expectations

"What were you hoping we might do for you today?" — management expectations and desired outcomes

03
Past Medical & Surgical History
PMH, PSH, hospitalisations — build the medical timeline
▾
  • 📖"Have you had any significant illnesses in the past?" — prompt with MJ THREADS if needed
  • 🏥Previous hospitalisations, operations, procedures, blood transfusions
  • 🔪Surgical history: type of surgery, when, complications, anaesthetic issues
  • ⚠️Any previous similar episodes? Diagnosed conditions? Regular follow-up?
MJ THREADS — Common Conditions to Screen
M — MI / Cardiac
Angina, MI, heart failure, arrhythmias, rheumatic fever
J — Jaundice / Liver
Hepatitis, cirrhosis, gallstones
T — TB / Respiratory
TB, asthma, COPD, pneumonia
H — Hypertension
BP, hyperlipidaemia, stroke, TIA
R — Rheumatic/Renal
Arthritis, joint problems, kidney disease
E — Epilepsy / Neuro
Seizures, blackouts, headaches, neurological conditions
A — Anaemia/Blood
Anaemia, bleeding disorders, clotting problems, DVT/PE
D — Diabetes
Type 1, Type 2, gestational, complications
S — Stroke/Surgery
Any previous surgeries, hospitalisations, procedures
04
Drug History & Allergies
Medications — prescribed, OTC, herbal, illicit; allergies with reactions
▾
  • 💊"Are you taking any medications?" — name, dose, frequency, how long, compliance
  • 🌿"Any over-the-counter medications? Herbal remedies? Vitamins/supplements?"
  • 💉"Any recreational drugs? Contraceptive pill?" — ask non-judgementally
  • ⚠️Allergies: "Do you have any allergies?" — Always ask: DRUG, food, environmental. Document: allergen + reaction type (anaphylaxis vs rash vs intolerance)
  • 🔄Recent changes to medications? Any new drugs started before symptoms began?
🚨 Allergy vs intolerance — critical distinction: Nausea/GI upset = intolerance (NOT a true allergy). Urticaria, angioedema, bronchospasm, anaphylaxis = TRUE allergy. Mislabelling causes under-treatment and medication errors.
05
Family History
Three generations — construct a family tree if relevant
▾
  • 👨‍👩‍👧"Is there any history of similar problems in the family?" — parents, siblings, grandparents, children
  • 💔Cardiac disease: MI at young age (<55 yrs in males, <65 in females) → familial hypercholesterolaemia, HCM
  • 🎗️Cancer: colorectal (Lynch syndrome), breast/ovarian (BRCA), gastric, lung
  • 🧬Genetic conditions: haemophilia, cystic fibrosis, Huntington's, sickle cell, thalassaemia
  • 🫀Autoimmune: diabetes, thyroid disease, SLE, IBD — tendency to run in families
  • 🧠Neurological: epilepsy, dementia (Alzheimer's, Huntington's), familial ataxias
  • ⚰️Age and cause of death of parents/siblings if deceased
06
Social History
SADMA — the context that shapes health and management
▾
SADMA Framework
S — Smoking
Current/ex/never. Pack-years = (packs/day × years). Smoking type (cigarettes, shisha, cannabis). Quit date if ex
A — Alcohol
Units/week (1 unit = 10ml/8g ethanol). Men <21 units/week, women <14. CAGE/AUDIT questionnaire. Type, binge pattern
D — Drugs
Illicit drug use — type, frequency, route (IV drug use → hepatitis, HIV, endocarditis risk)
M — Marital/Relationships
Married/partner/widowed/divorced. Dependants. Support network. Carer status
A — Activity/Occupation
Job (hazards — asbestos, silica, solvents, shift work). Exercise level. ADLs — can they manage independently?
Additional Social Factors
  • 🏠Living situation: House/flat, stairs, alone vs with family, ground floor? Sheltered accommodation? Care home?
  • ✈️Travel history: Recent international travel (malaria, TB, typhoid, Dengue, COVID-19) — specific regions, activities
  • 🐾Animal contact: Pets, farm animals, exotic animals (zoonoses — brucellosis, Q fever, psittacosis)
  • 🍽️Diet: Vegetarian/vegan (B12 deficiency), food restrictions, appetite changes, weight loss
07
Systems Review
Brief screen of all systems — don't miss relevant symptoms
▾
SystemKey Questions
CVSChest pain, palpitations, dyspnoea, orthopnoea, PND, ankle swelling, syncope, intermittent claudication
RespiratoryCough (productive?), haemoptysis, wheeze, dyspnoea, night sweats, pleuritic chest pain
GITNausea, vomiting, dysphagia, odynophagia, heartburn, abdominal pain, altered bowel habit, PR bleeding, melaena, weight loss, jaundice
UrinaryDysuria, frequency, nocturia, haematuria, hesitancy, poor stream, incontinence, loin pain
NeurologicalHeadache, visual changes, seizures, weakness, numbness, speech problems, memory, balance, blackouts
MSKJoint pain/swelling/stiffness (morning stiffness duration), muscle weakness, back pain, fractures
EndocrineThirst, polyuria, weight change, heat/cold intolerance, sweating, hair/skin/voice changes
HaematologicalFatigue, pallor, bruising, bleeding tendency, lymphadenopathy, night sweats, infections
Obs/Gynae (♀)LMP, cycle regularity, menorrhagia, dysmenorrhoea, pregnancy history, smear history, HRT
✅ Closing the consultation: "Is there anything else you'd like to tell me or ask me about?" — summarise your findings back to the patient, confirm understanding, explain next steps clearly.
🫃
GIT Examination
Gastrointestinal system — inspection, palpation, percussion, auscultation
01
Preparation & Peripheral Signs
Position patient correctly — peripheral stigmata of GI disease
▾
  • 🛏️Patient supine, one pillow, abdomen exposed from nipples to pubic symphysis. Expose legs later
  • 💡Ensure good tangential lighting — raking light reveals subtle contours
Hands (GI peripheries)
Clubbing
Cirrhosis, IBD (Crohn's), coeliac, GI lymphoma
Leukonychia
Hypoalbuminaemia → cirrhosis, malnutrition
Palmar erythema
Liver disease (oestrogen excess)
Dupuytren's contracture
Alcoholic liver disease
Asterixis
Hepatic encephalopathy → outstretched hands, coarse flap
Excoriations
Cholestatic jaundice (bile salt deposition → itch)
Face & Eyes
  • 🟡Jaundice (sclerae): Pre-hepatic (haemolysis — lemon yellow, dark urine absent, dark stools), hepatic (orange-yellow, dark urine, pale stools), post-hepatic (obstructive — deep yellow/green, dark urine, pale stools, itch)
  • ⬜Anaemia: Pallor of conjunctivae → GI blood loss (ulcer, cancer, IBD), malabsorption (coeliac, B12)
  • 🔵Parotid enlargement: Bilateral → alcoholic liver disease, eating disorders
  • 💧Mouth: Angular stomatitis (iron/B12), apthous ulcers (Crohn's, coeliac), glossitis, pigmentation (Peutz-Jeghers)
02
Inspection of the Abdomen
Look before you touch — 9 regions, all layers
▾
Inspect From the Side and Foot of Bed
  • 📐Contour: Scaphoid (malnutrition, dehydration), flat, protuberant (fat, flatus, fluid, faeces, fetus, fatal masses, fibroids)
  • 🔄Symmetry: Asymmetric distension → organomegaly, mass, localised obstruction
  • 🌊Movement with breathing: Normal = abdominal movement. Absent = peritonitis (patient doesn't breathe abdominally)
  • ✂️Scars: Note location → midline (laparotomy), RIF (appendicectomy), RUQ (cholecystectomy — laparoscopic ports), flank (nephrectomy), Pfannenstiel (gynaecology/Caesarean)
  • 💜Dilated veins: Caput medusae (portal hypertension — flow away from umbilicus). IVC obstruction (flow upward)
  • 🔵Visible peristalsis: Small bowel obstruction (thin patient), pyloric stenosis (infant)
  • 🟣Skin changes: Striae (pregnancy, Cushing's, rapid weight change), bruising (Grey-Turner = retroperitoneal bleed, Cullen's = periumbilical → haemoperitoneum — pancreatitis/ectopic)
  • 🫁Hernias: Umbilical, paraumbilical, inguinal, femoral — ask patient to cough
03
Palpation
Light then deep — 9 regions — watch the face!
▾
  • ⚠️ALWAYS ASK about pain first — start away from the painful area. Watch the patient's FACE, not your hand
  • 🖐️Light palpation first: All 9 regions. Assess: tenderness, guarding (voluntary vs involuntary), rigidity, superficial masses
  • ✊Deep palpation: Masses — site, size, shape, surface, consistency, edge, mobility, pulsatility, tenderness
Specific Palpation Signs
Guarding
Involuntary muscle rigidity = peritoneal irritation. Board-like rigidity = generalised peritonitis
Rebound tenderness
Pain on release > pressure = peritonism. Use percussion instead (kinder to patient)
Murphy's sign
Two fingers RUQ → patient inhales deeply → catches breath = acute cholecystitis (positive Murphy's)
Rovsing's sign
Palpation in LIF → pain in RIF = appendicitis (referred tenderness via peritoneal irritation)
McBurney's point
1/3 of the way from ASIS to umbilicus → maximal tenderness in appendicitis
Psoas sign
Extend hip against resistance → RIF pain = retrocaecal appendicitis or psoas abscess
04
Liver, Spleen & Kidney Examination
Organomegaly — palpate + percuss + measure
▾
Liver
  • ⬇️Start in RIF, move diagonally toward RUQ on inspiration — feel for firm lower edge
  • 📏Measure span by percussion: upper border (resonant→dull — 5th intercostal space MCL), lower border (dull→resonant). Normal span: 12–15 cm
  • 🔊Auscultate for bruit (hepatocellular carcinoma, AVM) or rub (perihepatitis, infarction)
Hepatomegaly — Causes
Cirrhosis, hepatitis, fatty liver, cardiac failure (nutmeg liver), haematological malignancy, hepatocellular carcinoma, metastases, hydatid cyst
Characteristics to report
Size (cm below costal margin), surface (smooth vs nodular), consistency (soft/firm/hard), tender, pulsatile (TR), bruit
Spleen
  • ⬆️Start in RIF, palpate toward LUQ — spleen enlarges diagonally toward right iliac fossa
  • 🔄Cannot get above it (goes under costal margin), notched medial border, dull to percussion, moves with respiration, NOT ballottable
  • 📐If not felt — confirm with percussion: Traube's space (9th–11th rib, midaxillary line) normally resonant. Dullness = splenomegaly
Splenomegaly Causes
Haematological: haemolytic anaemia, lymphoma, leukaemia, myelofibrosis. Infective: EBV, malaria, schistosomiasis. Portal HTN: cirrhosis. Autoimmune: SLE, RA (Felty's)
Massive Splenomegaly
Crosses midline to RIF. Causes: CML, myelofibrosis, malaria, visceral leishmaniasis (kala-azar), Gaucher's disease
Kidneys (Ballottement)
  • 🫙Bimanual technique: one hand posteriorly (push forward), one anteriorly (feel for ballot) — renal mass can be ballotted unlike spleen
  • 🔵Can get above kidney (unlike spleen), resonant to percussion (overlying bowel), does NOT move with respiration
05
Percussion & Ascites
Tympanic vs dull — shifting dullness — fluid thrill
▾
  • 🥁Normal abdomen: tympanic (hollow viscera containing gas). Dullness = solid organ or fluid
  • 📍Percuss systematically — all 9 regions. Note borders of organs
Ascites Detection

Shifting Dullness

Percuss from umbilicus toward flank — note where dullness begins. Hand stays still. Patient rolls away 45°. Wait 30 sec. Percuss again — dullness shifts (fluid moves with gravity). Sensitive: detects ~1500 mL of ascites

Fluid Thrill

One hand flat on one flank. Flick the other flank. Ask patient/assistant to press ulnar edge of hand along midline (stops fat wave). Feel a thrill transmitted = large volume ascites (>2L). Less sensitive than shifting dullness

Causes of Ascites (by SAAG)
SAAG ≥11 g/L (Transudate)SAAG <11 g/L (Exudate)
Liver cirrhosis (most common), cardiac failure, Budd-Chiari, portal vein thrombosisMalignancy (peritoneal mets), TB peritonitis, pancreatitis, nephrotic syndrome
06
Auscultation & Completion
Bowel sounds, bruits, hernia orifices, PR exam
▾
Bowel Sounds
  • 🔊Listen in one area for 2 minutes before declaring absent. Normal = intermittent gurgles every 5–30 sec
  • 📢Hyperactive / tinkling: Mechanical bowel obstruction (intermittent, high-pitched), gastroenteritis, early obstruction
  • 🔇Absent: Paralytic ileus (post-op, peritonitis, electrolyte disturbance, drugs)
Bruits
  • 🩺Aorta (epigastrium) — aortic aneurysm. Renal arteries (lateral to midline) — renal artery stenosis (renovascular HTN). Hepatic — HCC, AVM. Splenic — splenic artery aneurysm
To Complete
  • 🦵Examine hernial orifices (inguinal, femoral) — ask to cough, check reducibility
  • 🫀Check for external genitalia (scrotal swelling, varicocele in males)
  • 🩻Offer digital rectal examination (DRE) — essential for PR bleeding, constipation, prostate
  • 🌡️Check temperature chart, urine dipstick. Review LFTs, amylase, lipase, U&E as indicated
🚨 Never forget DRE in rectal bleeding, change in bowel habit, or suspected appendicitis! A rectal carcinoma is palpable in ~75% of cases. PR blood on the glove = crucial finding.
🦴
Rheumatology Examination
GALS screen + regional joint examination — look, feel, move
01
GALS Screen
Gait, Arms, Legs, Spine — the rheumatological screening tool
▾
3 Screening Questions First
  • ❓"Do you have any pain or stiffness in your muscles, joints or back?"
  • ❓"Can you dress yourself completely without any difficulty?"
  • ❓"Can you walk up and down stairs without any difficulty?"
GALS Assessment
RegionWhat to AssessNormal Response
GaitWalk to far wall and back, turn, walk back. Observe all phasesSmooth, symmetric, heel strike, toe-off, arm swing
ArmsHands: spread fingers, make fist, grip, fine pinch. Elbow: fully extend. Shoulder: hands behind head (abduct + ER). TMJ: open mouth 3 fingersFull range, no swelling, no pain
LegsHips: flex/ER/IR (supine). Knee: flex, extend, patellar tap/effusion. Ankle + MTP: squeeze across joints. Foot inspectionFull range, no effusion, no tenderness
SpineCervical: rotate, flex/extend. Lumbar: fingertip-to-floor (measure), modified Schober's. Thoracic kyphosis. ScoliosisFull ROM, >5 cm Schober's, no kyphoscoliosis
02
Hand & Wrist Examination
Rheumatoid vs Osteoarthritis vs Psoriatic — key differentials
▾
Look — Feel — Move
  • 👁️Inspect dorsum then palmar surface: Swelling (which joints?), deformity, skin changes, muscle wasting, nail changes (pitting, onycholysis → psoriasis)
  • ✋Palpate: MCP joints (squeeze), PIP joints (squeeze), wrist, DIP joints — warmth, tenderness, swelling type (bony = OA, soft = RA)
  • 🔄Active then passive movement: Wrist extension/flexion, MCP/PIP/DIP flexion-extension, grip strength, fine pinch
Differential Diagnosis by Deformity
DeformityDescriptionDiagnosis
Ulnar deviationMCPs deviated ulnarlyRheumatoid arthritis
Swan neckPIP hyperextension + DIP flexionRheumatoid arthritis, SLE
BoutonnièrePIP flexion + DIP hyperextensionRheumatoid arthritis
Z-thumbThumb IP hyperextension + MCP flexionRheumatoid arthritis
Heberden's nodesBony swelling at DIP jointsOsteoarthritis
Bouchard's nodesBony swelling at PIP jointsOsteoarthritis
Sausage digitsDactylitis — whole digit swollenPsoriatic arthritis, reactive arthritis
Tophaceous goutChalky white deposits over jointsChronic tophaceous gout
03
Knee Examination
Effusion tests, ligament stability, meniscal tests
▾
Inspection
  • 👁️Standing: genu valgum (knock-knee), genu varum (bow-legged), deformity, wasting of quadriceps (especially VMO)
  • 🛏️Supine: swelling (supra/infrapatellar), erythema, scars, skin changes
Effusion Tests

Patellar Tap

Empty suprapatellar pouch with one hand (slide from 10 cm above). With other hand, press patella sharply down. A "tap" felt = moderate-large effusion (>15 mL)

Bulge Sign (Stroke Test)

Stroke medial side of knee up and out (empties medial gutter). Quickly stroke lateral side — fluid bulge appears on medial side. Small effusion (<10 mL)

Key Ligament Tests
Anterior Drawer / Lachman
ACL — tibial anterior translation at 90° (drawer) or 20° flexion (Lachman, more sensitive)
Posterior Drawer
PCL — posterior tibial sag at 90° (posterior sagging sign)
Valgus / Varus Stress
MCL (valgus force) and LCL (varus force) — test at 30° flexion
McMurray's Test
Flex knee fully, foot externally/internally rotated, extend → click/pain = meniscal tear
04
Hip Examination
FABER, FADIR, Trendelenburg, Thomas test
▾
Key Hip Tests
Thomas Test
Flex opposite hip fully (flattens lumbar lordosis). Affected leg rises off bed = fixed flexion deformity. Measure angle
FABER (Patrick's) Test
Figure-4 position — hip Flexion, ABduction, External Rotation. Pain = hip joint pathology or sacroiliac joint disease
FADIR Test
Hip Flexion, ADduction, Internal Rotation → pain = femoroacetabular impingement (FAI), hip labral tear
Trendelenburg Test
Patient stands on one leg — pelvis drops on opposite side = positive. Gluteus medius weakness (hip OA, superior gluteal nerve palsy, DDH)
Log Roll
Supine, rotate entire limb passively — pain/restriction = hip joint disease (capsular pattern: IR>flexion>ER)
Leg Length
True (ASIS to medial malleolus) vs apparent (umbilicus to medial malleolus). True shortening = hip/femur pathology
⚠️ Referred pain to the knee from the hip is common — always examine the hip if a patient presents with knee pain and no local knee cause is found. Especially in children (Perthes', SUFE — hip pathology presents as knee pain).
05
Differentiating Arthritis Patterns
RA vs OA vs Psoriatic vs Gout vs Reactive
▾
FeatureRheumatoid (RA)OsteoarthritisPsoriaticGout
JointsMCP, PIP, wrists, knees, C-spine. SymmetricalDIP, CMC thumb, hips, knees, L-spineDIP, asymmetric, axial1st MTP (podagra), ankles, knees
SwellingSoft, boggy (synovitis)Bony (osteophytes)Dactylitis, enthesitisHot, red, shiny, exquisitely tender
StiffnessMorning >1 hourMorning <30 min, worse with useMorning stiffnessAcute onset at night
Extra-articularNodules, anaemia, vasculitis, lung, eyeCrepitus, deformityPsoriatic skin/nail changesTophi, renal stones, uric acid
LabsRF+, anti-CCP+, raised CRP/ESRNormalRF−, HLA-B27 (axial)↑Uric acid, negatively birefringent crystals on aspiration
💡 Septic arthritis is a rheumatological emergency — red, hot, swollen single joint with fever and systemic upset. Joint aspiration mandatory (neutrophils >50,000/µL). Do NOT delay antibiotics. Most common organism: Staphylococcus aureus. Never miss in immunocompromised patients.
